abstract:
Robotic grasping is a cornerstone capability of embodied systems. Many
methods directly output grasps from partial information without modeling the
geometry of the scene, leading to suboptimal motion and even collisions. To
address these issues, we introduce ZeroGrasp, a novel framework that
simultaneously performs 3D reconstruction and grasp pose prediction in near
real-time. A key insight of our method is that occlusion reasoning and modeling
the spatial relationships between objects is beneficial for both accurate
reconstruction and grasping. We couple our method with a novel large-scale
synthetic dataset, which comprises 1M photo-realistic images, high-resolution
3D reconstructions and 11.3B physically-valid grasp pose annotations for 12K
objects from the Objaverse-LVIS dataset. We evaluate ZeroGrasp on the
GraspNet-1B benchmark as well as through real-world robot experiments.
ZeroGrasp achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes to novel
real-world objects by leveraging synthetic data.

keyInsights: Intermediate network layers through multi-object encoding improve both reconstruction and grasp pose quality
Synthetic training data can enable effective zero-shot transfer to real-world objects
Explicit geometry modeling enables better collision avoidance and stable grasping
originalProblem: Existing grasp detection methods often output grasps without explicitly modeling object geometry, leading to collisions and unstable grasps
Limited availability of datasets with ground-truth 3D shapes and grasp pose annotations for training robust grasping models
results: State-of-the-art performance on GraspNet-1B benchmark for robotic grasping
Successful generalization to novel real-world objects in robot experiments
Near real-time performance suitable for practical robotic applications
Two new large-scale datasets contributed to advance research in the field
solution: Unified framework coupling 3D reconstruction and grasp pose prediction using octree-based conditional variational autoencoder
Multi-object encoder and 3D occlusion fields to model spatial relationships and handle occlusions
Two new datasets: ReOcS for evaluating reconstruction under occlusion and ZeroGrasp-11B for training
paper_summary.summary: A framework from Carnegie Mellon and Toyota Research Institute enables zero-shot robotic grasping by combining octree-based 3D shape reconstruction with grasp pose prediction from single RGB-D images, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the GraspNet-1B benchmark while generalizing to novel real-world objects through training on synthetic data.

title: ZeroGrasp: Zero-Shot Shape Reconstruction Enabled Robotic Grasping

abstract:
Bimanual manipulation is essential in robotics, yet developing foundation
models is extremely challenging due to the inherent complexity of coordinating
two robot arms (leading to multi-modal action distributions) and the scarcity
of training data. In this paper, we present the Robotics Diffusion Transformer
(RDT), a pioneering diffusion foundation model for bimanual manipulation. RDT
builds on diffusion models to effectively represent multi-modality, with
innovative designs of a scalable Transformer to deal with the heterogeneity of
multi-modal inputs and to capture the nonlinearity and high frequency of
robotic data. To address data scarcity, we further introduce a Physically
Interpretable Unified Action Space, which can unify the action representations
of various robots while preserving the physical meanings of original actions,
facilitating learning transferrable physical knowledge. With these designs, we
managed to pre-train RDT on the largest collection of multi-robot datasets to
date and scaled it up to 1.2B parameters, which is the largest diffusion-based
foundation model for robotic manipulation. We finally fine-tuned RDT on a
self-created multi-task bimanual dataset with over 6K+ episodes to refine its
manipulation capabilities. Experiments on real robots demonstrate that RDT
significantly outperforms existing methods. It exhibits zero-shot
generalization to unseen objects and scenes, understands and follows language
instructions, learns new skills with just 1~5 demonstrations, and effectively
handles complex, dexterous tasks. We refer to
this https URL for the code and videos.

keyInsights: Diffusion models are effective for capturing and sampling from multi-modal action distributions in complex bimanual tasks, avoiding "average" and infeasible actions.
Large-scale cross-robot pre-training on diverse datasets, combined with a unified action space, is crucial for overcoming data scarcity and enabling robust generalization in bimanual manipulation.

Architectural adaptations to Diffusion Transformers (e.g., QKNorm, RMSNorm, non-linear MLP decoder, alternating condition injection) are essential for stable scaling and handling the unique, high-frequency, and non-linear properties of robotic data.
originalProblem: Developing generalizable bimanual manipulation policies is challenging due to the inherent complexity of coordinating two arms for diverse tasks.
Data scarcity for dual-arm robot systems hinders the training of data-hungry foundation models.
Bimanual tasks often exhibit multi-modal action distributions, which traditional imitation learning struggles to capture, potentially leading to physically infeasible "average" actions.
results: RDT-1B achieved an average 56% improvement in success rates compared to state-of-the-art baselines across a wide range of challenging bimanual tasks on a real ALOHA dual-arm robot.

The model demonstrated exceptional zero-shot generalization, successfully performing tasks with unseen objects, scenes, and novel language instructions (e.g., precise pouring volumes) without prior exposure.
RDT-1B exhibited strong few-shot learning, adapting to complex new skills from as few as 1-5 demonstrations, significantly outperforming baselines which failed.
solution: Introduces RDT-1B, a 1.2-billion-parameter Diffusion Transformer (DiT) model, adapted with QKNorm, RMSNorm, MLP Decoder, and Alternating Condition Injection for stable large-scale training on robotic data.
Employs a "Physically Interpretable Unified Action Space" to map heterogeneous robot actions into a common 128-dimensional representation, enabling extensive cross-robot pre-training.

Utilizes a pre-training and fine-tuning pipeline: pre-trains on over 1 million trajectories from 46 diverse multi-robot datasets, then fine-tunes on a self-collected bimanual dataset of 6,000 trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Tsinghua University developed RDT-1B, the first diffusion-based foundation model for bimanual robotic manipulation, featuring 1.2 billion parameters. This model achieved an average 56% improvement in success rates over state-of-the-art baselines across diverse bimanual tasks, showcasing strong zero-shot generalization to unseen objects and scenes, and few-shot learning capabilities.

title: RDT-1B: a Diffusion Foundation Model for Bimanual Manipulation

abstract:
Human hands play a central role in interacting, motivating increasing
research in dexterous robotic manipulation. Data-driven embodied AI algorithms
demand precise, large-scale, human-like manipulation sequences, which are
challenging to obtain with conventional reinforcement learning or real-world
teleoperation. To address this, we introduce ManipTrans, a novel two-stage
method for efficiently transferring human bimanual skills to dexterous robotic
hands in simulation. ManipTrans first pre-trains a generalist trajectory
imitator to mimic hand motion, then fine-tunes a specific residual module under
interaction constraints, enabling efficient learning and accurate execution of
complex bimanual tasks. Experiments show that ManipTrans surpasses
state-of-the-art methods in success rate, fidelity, and efficiency. Leveraging
ManipTrans, we transfer multiple hand-object datasets to robotic hands,
creating DexManipNet, a large-scale dataset featuring previously unexplored
tasks like pen capping and bottle unscrewing. DexManipNet comprises 3.3K
episodes of robotic manipulation and is easily extensible, facilitating further
policy training for dexterous hands and enabling real-world deployments.

keyInsights: Separating trajectory imitation from physical interaction handling improves transfer efficiency
Residual learning effectively bridges the gap between initial imitation and required physical adjustments
Framework generalizes across different robotic hand designs through embodiment-agnostic approach
originalProblem: Traditional approaches to teaching robots complex manipulation skills rely on labor-intensive teleoperation or struggle with direct transfer of human motion data
Existing methods face challenges in handling high-dimensional action spaces and morphological differences between human and robot hands
results: Successfully transferred complex bimanual tasks like bottle unscrewing across multiple robotic hand types
Generated 3.3K manipulation episodes (1.34M frames) in DEXMANIPNET dataset
Demonstrated real-world feasibility through deployment on physical robotic systems
Outperformed existing methods in success rate and motion fidelity benchmarks
solution: Two-stage transfer framework combining pre-trained trajectory imitation with residual learning for physical interactions
Curriculum learning strategy that gradually introduces physical constraints while maintaining motion fidelity
Generation of large-scale manipulation dataset (DEXMANIPNET) for training and benchmarking
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from BIGAI and Tsinghua University introduce MANIPTRANS, a two-stage framework that enables efficient transfer of human bimanual manipulation skills to robotic hands through combined trajectory imitation and residual learning, while generating DEXMANIPNET - a dataset of 3.3K manipulation episodes demonstrating generalization across multiple robotic hand designs.

title: ManipTrans: Efficient Dexterous Bimanual Manipulation Transfer via  Residual Learning

abstract:
A dexterous hand capable of grasping any object is essential for the
development of general-purpose embodied intelligent robots. However, due to the
high degree of freedom in dexterous hands and the vast diversity of objects,
generating high-quality, usable grasping poses in a robust manner is a
significant challenge. In this paper, we introduce DexGrasp Anything, a method
that effectively integrates physical constraints into both the training and
sampling phases of a diffusion-based generative model, achieving
state-of-the-art performance across nearly all open datasets. Additionally, we
present a new dexterous grasping dataset containing over 3.4 million diverse
grasping poses for more than 15k different objects, demonstrating its potential
to advance universal dexterous grasping. The code of our method and our dataset
will be publicly released soon.

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating physics-based constraints into diffusion models is crucial for generating physically plausible and stable robotic grasps, outperforming methods that rely solely on learned data distributions.
Leveraging large-scale, diverse, and high-quality datasets, coupled with a model-in-the-loop generation strategy, substantially improves generalization and diversity in dexterous grasping.
Fusing geometric object understanding with high-level semantic priors from Large Language Models enhances the model's ability to generate more effective and context-aware grasps.
originalProblem: Generating diverse, high-quality grasp poses for high-DoF dexterous hands across varied objects is challenging due to large search spaces and the difficulty of satisfying physical constraints.
Existing data-driven generative models often produce physically implausible grasps, manifesting as hand-object penetration or insufficient contact, which reduces real-world success rates.
The limited scale, diversity, and quality of existing datasets hinder the robustness and generalization capabilities of data-driven dexterous grasping methods on novel objects.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on five challenging dexterous grasping datasets, consistently showing higher success rates (e.g., 57.5% Suc.6 on DexGraspNet compared to UGG's 46.9%) and lower penetration rates.
Ablation studies confirmed the cumulative positive impact of individual physical constraints and physics-guided sampling, with LLM integration further boosting performance.

Models trained on the DGA dataset exhibited significantly increased grasp diversity (often more than doubling) while maintaining or improving success rates and generalization across different objects and datasets, and demonstrated successful grasps on a real ShadowHand robot.
solution: Introduces a physics-aware diffusion generator that integrates three explicit physical constraints (surface pulling, external penetration repulsion, self-penetration repulsion) during both training and sampling phases.
Employs an LLM-enhanced object representation by fusing geometric features from object point clouds with semantic priors derived from Large Language Models.

Constructs DexGrasp Anything (DGA), a large-scale, diverse dexterous grasping dataset of over 3.4 million poses on more than 15,000 distinct objects, using a multi-stage approach including curated data and a novel model-in-the-loop generation strategy with rigorous filtering.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at ShanghaiTech University developed DexGrasp Anything, a physics-aware diffusion model that generates dexterous grasp poses by integrating explicit physical constraints and leveraging an LLM-enhanced object representation. This method, supported by the newly introduced large-scale DexGrasp Anything (DGA) dataset, achieved state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and successfully demonstrated stable grasping with a real ShadowHand robot.

title: DexGrasp Anything: Towards Universal Robotic Dexterous Grasping with  Physics Awareness

abstract: Generating large-scale demonstrations for dexterous hand manipulation remains challenging, and several approaches have been proposed in recent years to address this. Among them, generative models have emerged as a promising paradigm, enabling the efficient creation of diverse and physically plausible demonstrations. In this paper, we introduce Dex1B, a large-scale, diverse, and high-quality demonstration dataset produced with generative models. The dataset contains one billion demonstrations for two fundamental tasks: grasping and articulation. To construct it, we propose a generative model that integrates geometric constraints to improve feasibility and applies additional conditions to enhance diversity. We validate the model on both established and newly introduced simulation benchmarks, where it significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, we demonstrate its effectiveness and robustness through real-world robot experiments. Our project page is at this https URL

keyInsights: The full potential of dexterous robotic hands can be unlocked by providing access to abundant, high-quality, and diverse demonstration data for learning manipulation policies.

A hybrid approach that merges the precision of optimization with the scalability and diversity-expansion capabilities of generative models offers an effective paradigm for synthetic data generation in robotics.

Embedding geometric constraints directly into generative model training, combined with fast post-optimization, is crucial for producing physically feasible and successful manipulation trajectories for robots.
originalProblem: Dexterous manipulation remains a complex challenge in robotics due to the high degrees of freedom of multi-fingered hands, making precise control and planning difficult.
A critical lack of sufficiently large-scale, high-quality, and diverse datasets has hindered the development of robust and generalizable learning-based policies for dexterous hands.
Existing generative models for robot data generation often struggle to produce physically plausible (feasible) demonstrations and tend to interpolate within their training data, limiting diversity.
results: Dex1B, a dataset of one billion demonstrations for grasping and articulation across over 6,000 objects and three dexterous hands, was constructed, exhibiting significantly higher diversity compared to human-annotated datasets.
DexSimple, trained on Dex1B, achieved an 86.0% success rate and a Q1-score of 0.125 on the DexGraspNet benchmark, establishing a new performance baseline by surpassing prior state-of-the-art methods.

Policies learned from Dex1B demonstrated superior generalization across lifting and articulation tasks, consistently outperforming those trained on human-annotated datasets, and achieved a 96% success rate in direct sim-to-real transfer on physical robots with unseen objects.
solution: An iterative data generation pipeline was developed, combining an efficient optimization method to create a high-quality seed dataset with a generative model (DexSimple) for massive data scaling.

DexSimple, a Conditional Variational Autoencoder, incorporates geometric constraints (SDF-based loss) during training and employs a lightweight post-optimization step to ensure the physical plausibility and success of generated hand poses.

A debiasing strategy, which prioritizes sampling actions for underrepresented object regions or types, was integrated to explicitly expand the diversity of generated demonstrations beyond initial data patterns.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UC San Diego introduced Dex1B, a dataset comprising one billion physically plausible demonstrations for dexterous grasping and articulation tasks, utilizing an iterative data generation pipeline. Policies trained on Dex1B achieve state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks and demonstrate high success rates in direct sim-to-real deployment on physical robots.

title: Dex1B: Learning with 1B Demonstrations for Dexterous Manipulation

abstract: Recent advances in dexterous grasping synthesis have demonstrated significant progress in producing reasonable and plausible grasps for many task purposes. But it remains challenging to generalize to unseen object categories and diverse task instructions. In this paper, we propose G-DexGrasp, a retrieval-augmented generation approach that can produce high-quality dexterous hand configurations for unseen object categories and language-based task instructions. The key is to retrieve generalizable grasping priors, including the fine-grained contact part and the affordance-related distribution of relevant grasping instances, for the following synthesis pipeline. Specifically, the fine-grained contact part and affordance act as generalizable guidance to infer reasonable grasping configurations for unseen objects with a generative model, while the relevant grasping distribution plays as regularization to guarantee the plausibility of synthesized grasps during the subsequent refinement optimization. Our comparison experiments validate the effectiveness of our key designs for generalization and demonstrate the remarkable performance against the existing approaches. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Leveraging a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) strategy, combining MLLM-based semantic understanding with data retrieval, enables generalization of dexterous grasping to unseen object categories.
Part-aware priors, specifically localized contact parts and their associated grasping distributions, are crucial for guiding both the initial generation of hand poses and their subsequent refinement.
A two-stage optimization process, first refining extrinsic parameters then all parameters, guided by retrieved priors, effectively improves the precision and physical feasibility of generated grasps.
originalProblem: Existing task-oriented dexterous grasping methods lack generalization ability to novel object categories, struggling to produce functional grasps for objects not seen during training.
Generating precise dexterous hand configurations for diverse, unstructured natural language instructions, especially when combined with unseen objects, remains a challenge.
The high degrees of freedom in dexterous hands lead to a vast search space, making direct generation for novel objects without specific guidance difficult and prone to errors.
results: G-DexGrasp significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on unseen object categories across metrics like Solid Intersection Volume (2.94 cm³ vs. 7.98 cm³), Penetration Depth (0.29 cm vs. 0.92 cm), and Part Accuracy (71.6% vs. 46.5%).

Ablation studies confirm the necessity of the part-aware grasping generation network for providing robust initial poses and the prior-guided optimization for achieving precise, stable, and semantically aligned grasps.
The MLLM (GPT-4o) achieved high accuracy (96.99%) in grasp-type parsing and reasonable precision (76.6%) in combined part parsing and grounding, validating its role in semantic understanding.
solution: A three-stage Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) strategy is employed: Generalizable Grasping Prior Retrieval, Part-Aware Grasping Generation Network, and Prior-Guided Grasping Optimization.
MLLMs analyze task instructions to infer affordance types and contact part names, which are then localized on the 3D object to retrieve generalizable grasping priors from a dataset.

A CVAE generates coarse hand configurations based on the retrieved priors and localized contact parts, followed by a differentiable grasp optimizer that refines parameters using retrieved prior guidance and physical feasibility constraints.
paper_summary.summary: G-DexGrasp synthesizes dexterous hand configurations for novel objects and diverse language instructions by integrating Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for semantic understanding with a retrieval-augmented generation strategy. It outperforms existing methods on unseen object categories across stability, penetration, and semantic alignment metrics, demonstrating robust generalization.

title: G-DexGrasp: Generalizable Dexterous Grasping Synthesis Via Part-Aware Prior Retrieval and Prior-Assisted Generation

abstract: Simulation-based data synthesis has emerged as a powerful paradigm for advancing real-world robotic manipulation. Yet existing datasets remain insufficient for robust bimanual manipulation due to (1) the lack of scalable task generation methods and (2) oversimplified simulation environments. We present RoboTwin 2.0, a scalable framework for automated, large-scale generation of diverse and realistic data, together with unified evaluation protocols for dual-arm manipulation. At its core is RoboTwin-OD, an object library of 731 instances across 147 categories with semantic and manipulation-relevant annotations. Building on this, we design an expert data synthesis pipeline that leverages multimodal language models (MLLMs) and simulation-in-the-loop refinement to automatically generate task-level execution code. To improve sim-to-real transfer, RoboTwin 2.0 applies structured domain randomization along five axes: clutter, lighting, background, tabletop height, and language, enhancing data diversity and policy robustness. The framework is instantiated across 50 dual-arm tasks and five robot embodiments. Empirically, it yields a 10.9% gain in code generation success rate. For downstream policy learning, a VLA model trained with synthetic data plus only 10 real demonstrations achieves a 367% relative improvement over the 10-demo baseline, while zero-shot models trained solely on synthetic data obtain a 228% gain. These results highlight the effectiveness of RoboTwin 2.0 in strengthening sim-to-real transfer and robustness to environmental variations. We release the data generator, benchmark, dataset, and code to support scalable research in robust bimanual manipulation. Project Page: this https URL, Code: this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
Southern University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Multimodal feedback from a vision-language model, combined with execution logs, substantially improves the reliability and success rate of MLLM-generated robotic task code.

Extensive and systematic domain randomization across visual, physical, and linguistic factors is critical for training policies that exhibit strong robustness and generalization to real-world complexities.

Accounting for robot-specific kinematic differences through embodiment-aware grasp adaptation is essential for efficient manipulation across diverse robotic platforms, especially those with fewer degrees of freedom.
originalProblem: The prohibitive cost and complexity of collecting large-scale, high-quality real-world datasets for bimanual robotic tasks.
Existing synthetic datasets often lack automated quality control, leading to execution failures or suboptimal grasps that hinder policy learning.
Current simulation environments employ superficial domain randomization, oversimplifying real-world complexities and limiting robust sim-to-real transfer.
results: Automated expert code generation achieved an average success rate of 71.3% with multimodal feedback, alongside reduced token costs for code refinement.
Policies trained with RoboTwin 2.0's domain-randomized data showed relative improvements of up to 31.9% in average success rate for generalization on unseen, randomized conditions in simulation.

Real-world experiments demonstrated policies augmented with RoboTwin 2.0 data achieved a 24.4% improvement in average real-world success rate in few-shot scenarios and 21.0% in zero-shot generalization on unseen backgrounds.
solution: An automated expert data generation pipeline leveraging multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and closed-loop simulation-in-the-loop feedback to synthesize and refine high-quality task execution code.
Comprehensive domain randomization systematically applied across five dimensions: scene clutter, background textures, lighting variations, tabletop heights, and diverse language instructions.
Embodiment-aware grasp adaptation mechanisms that account for heterogeneous dual-arm kinematics, generating robot-specific action candidates and manipulation strategies.
paper_summary.summary: RoboTwin 2.0 introduces a scalable simulation framework and benchmark designed to generate high-quality, domain-randomized data for robust bimanual robotic manipulation, addressing limitations in existing synthetic datasets. Policies trained with RoboTwin 2.0 data achieved a 24.4% improvement in real-world success rates for few-shot learning and 21.0% for zero-shot generalization on unseen backgrounds.

title: RoboTwin 2.0: A Scalable Data Generator and Benchmark with Strong Domain Randomization for Robust Bimanual Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Generalizable dexterous grasping with suitable grasp types is a fundamental skill for intelligent robots. Developing such skills requires a large-scale and high-quality dataset that covers numerous grasp types (i.e., at least those categorized by the GRASP taxonomy), but collecting such data is extremely challenging. Existing automatic grasp synthesis methods are often limited to specific grasp types or object categories, hindering scalability. This work proposes an efficient pipeline capable of synthesizing contact-rich, penetration-free, and physically plausible grasps for any grasp type, object, and articulated hand. Starting from a single human-annotated template for each hand and grasp type, our pipeline tackles the complicated synthesis problem with two stages: optimize the object to fit the hand template first, and then locally refine the hand to fit the object in simulation. To validate the synthesized grasps, we introduce a contact-aware control strategy that allows the hand to apply the appropriate force at each contact point to the object. Those validated grasps can also be used as new grasp templates to facilitate future synthesis. Experiments show that our method significantly outperforms previous type-unaware grasp synthesis baselines in simulation. Using our algorithm, we construct a dataset containing 10.7k objects and 9.5M grasps, covering 31 grasp types in the GRASP taxonomy. Finally, we train a type-conditional generative model that successfully performs the desired grasp type from single-view object point clouds, achieving an 82.3% success rate in real-world experiments. Project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Combining parallelized global object-centric alignment with local hand-centric refinement in a differentiable physics environment efficiently generates high-quality, type-aware grasps.
The iterative self-improvement of the grasp template library significantly enhances synthesis success rates and generalizability by continuously learning from successfully generated grasps.
Rigorous, contact-aware simulation validation and a unified grasp quality metric are crucial for ensuring the physical plausibility and stability of synthesized grasps for real-world application.
originalProblem: Robots struggle with 'true dexterous grasping,' which requires selecting and executing appropriate grasp types for specific tasks, moving beyond mere object acquisition.
Creating large-scale, high-quality, and type-aware datasets for multi-fingered hands is challenging, hindering the development of data-driven learning approaches for dexterous manipulation.

Existing grasp synthesis methods often lack the ability to generate a diverse range of grasp types, ensure physical plausibility, or generalize effectively across various objects and hand configurations.
results: A large-scale Dexonomy dataset was constructed, containing 9.5 million grasps for 10,700 objects across 31 GRASP taxonomy types, synthesized efficiently in less than three days using 8 NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPUs.

The pipeline achieved a 60.50% grasp success rate on a fingertip grasp benchmark, outperforming existing baselines such as BODex (49.23%) and demonstrating superior contact richness with zero penetration.

A type-conditional generative model trained on the Dexonomy dataset achieved an 82.3% real-world success rate for type-aware grasping with a Shadow hand on 13 unseen objects, demonstrating the practical utility of the generated data.
solution: A two-stage optimization pipeline is introduced, beginning with lightweight global alignment of the object's pose to a human-annotated grasp template, followed by simulation-based local refinement of the hand's pose.

The pipeline leverages differentiable physics simulations (Warp and MuJoCo) for efficient optimization and rigorous validation, ensuring contact-rich, penetration-free, and physically plausible grasps.

A self-improving template library mechanism iteratively enhances the synthesis process by incorporating successful grasps as new templates, broadening the range of graspable objects and improving robustness.
paper_summary.summary: Dexonomy presents an efficient, two-stage pipeline for synthesizing 9.5 million physically plausible, type-aware dexterous grasps across 10,700 objects and 31 grasp types in under three days. The work further demonstrates training a generative model on this comprehensive dataset, achieving an 82.3% real-world success rate for type-conditional grasping with a Shadow hand.

title: Dexonomy: Synthesizing All Dexterous Grasp Types in a Grasp Taxonomy

abstract: Bimanual manipulation is essential for achieving human-like dexterity in robots, but the large-scale and diverse bimanual robot datasets remain scarce due to hardware heterogeneity across robotic platforms. To address the challenge, we present RoboCOIN, a comprehensive multi-embodiment bimanual manipulation dataset with over 180,000 demonstrations collected from 15 distinct robotic platforms. The dataset covers 16 scenarios, including residential, commercial, and working environments, with 421 tasks systematically organized by bimanual coordination patterns and object properties. Our key innovation is a hierarchical capability pyramid that provides multi-level annotations, spanning trajectory-level concepts, segment-level subtasks, and frame-level kinematics. We further develop CoRobot, a comprehensive processing framework featuring Robot Trajectory Markup Language (RTML) for quality assessment, automated annotation generation, and unified multi-embodiment management. Extensive experiments demonstrate the reliability and effectiveness of RoboCOIN in multi-embodiment bimanual learning, with significant performance improvements across various model architectures and robotic platforms. The complete dataset and framework are open-sourced and publicly available for further research purposes. Project website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Hierarchical Annotation Integration (HAI) substantially boosts VLA model performance, increasing success rates for complex bimanual tasks from 20% to 70% by providing layered contextual information.
Rigorous data quality control via RTML is critical, filtering out an average of 35.3% of low-quality trajectories and leading to a significant 16% to 23% improvement in robotic policy performance.

The combination of multi-embodiment data, structured annotations, and a robust data processing framework offers a foundational resource for developing more generalist and dexterous bimanual robotic policies.
originalProblem: A scarcity of large-scale, diverse, and richly structured datasets for bimanual robotic manipulation hindered the development of generalist policies.
Hardware heterogeneity across numerous robotic platforms presented challenges for unified data collection and model training.
Existing datasets lacked deep structural understanding beyond raw action trajectories, limiting advanced reasoning and generalization capabilities in robot learning models.
results: The hierarchical annotation scheme improved VLA model success rates on a half-humanoid robot, raising simple task success from 80% to 90% and complex task success from 20% to 70%.
RTML filtering identified and removed 35.3% of low-quality trajectories, with grasping failures (52.7%) and velocity violations (46.2%) being the most common issues in human teleoperation.

Training the GR00T-N1.5 model with RTML-filtered data (GR00T-Fine) resulted in a 16% performance gain, and further augmenting with high-quality segments (GR00T-Mine) achieved a total 23% improvement over unfiltered data.
solution: Developed RoboCOIN, an open-sourced dataset comprising over 180,000 bimanual demonstrations collected from 15 distinct robotic platforms across 421 tasks.
Introduced a 'hierarchical capability pyramid' for multi-level annotations (trajectory-level concepts, segment-level subtasks, frame-level kinematics) to enable structured robot learning.

Created CoRobot, an integrated data processing framework, which includes the Robot Trajectory Markup Language (RTML) for quality control and a semi-automatic annotation toolchain for efficient data management.
paper_summary.summary: RoboCOIN is introduced as an open-source, multi-embodiment dataset for bimanual robotic manipulation, developed by researchers from BAAI and various institutions, providing over 180,000 demonstrations across diverse platforms. Its hierarchical annotations and CoRobot data quality framework enhance VLA model performance on complex bimanual tasks, with up to a 23% increase in success rates.

title: RoboCOIN: An Open-Sourced Bimanual Robotic Data COllection for INtegrated Manipulation

abstract: Grasping is a fundamental robot skill, yet despite significant research advancements, learning-based 6-DOF grasping approaches are still not turnkey and struggle to generalize across different embodiments and in-the-wild settings. We build upon the recent success on modeling the object-centric grasp generation process as an iterative diffusion process. Our proposed framework, GraspGen, consists of a DiffusionTransformer architecture that enhances grasp generation, paired with an efficient discriminator to score and filter sampled grasps. We introduce a novel and performant on-generator training recipe for the discriminator. To scale GraspGen to both objects and grippers, we release a new simulated dataset consisting of over 53 million grasps. We demonstrate that GraspGen outperforms prior methods in simulations with singulated objects across different grippers, achieves state-of-the-art performance on the FetchBench grasping benchmark, and performs well on a real robot with noisy visual observations.

keyInsights: Training the grasp discriminator on grasps generated by the diffusion model itself (on-generator training) effectively mitigates distribution shift, leading to a more accurate filter for the generator's specific failure modes.
Integrating a PointTransformerV3 encoder with a diffusion model enables high-quality grasp generation conditioned on object point clouds, outperforming traditional PointNet++ architectures.
An efficient discriminator architecture, reusing the frozen object encoder and directly processing SE(3) grasp representations, significantly reduces memory footprint while improving accuracy.
originalProblem: Current 6-DOF grasping systems struggle with generalization across diverse robot grippers, varying levels of sensory input (partial vs. complete point clouds), and complex cluttered environments.

Existing learning-based grasp generators often produce 'false positives' or sub-optimal grasps, and their associated discriminators frequently suffer from distribution shifts when trained on offline datasets.
A lack of large-scale, multi-gripper datasets hinders the development of truly general-purpose grasping models and robust sim-to-real transfer.
results: GraspGen achieved state-of-the-art results on the Franka-ACRONYM dataset (over 48% higher AUC than prior methods) and the FetchBench clutter benchmark (16.9% higher task success than Contact-GraspNet).
On-generator training for the discriminator improved its performance by 6.5% AUC compared to training on offline data, empirically confirming its effectiveness in handling distribution shifts.

Demonstrated strong sim-to-real transfer with an 81.3% real-world success rate for the Robotiq-2F-140 gripper, outperforming baselines and showing robust generalization across diverse real-world scenes and multiple gripper types.
solution: The GraspGen framework employs a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) to generate 6-DOF grasp poses, conditioned on object point clouds encoded by a PointTransformerV3 backbone.

It introduces a novel 'On-Generator Training' recipe for the grasp discriminator, where the discriminator is trained on grasps sampled and simulated from the diffusion model itself, improving its ability to filter specific generator failure modes.

A new, large-scale multi-gripper simulated dataset containing over 53 million grasps from Objaverse objects is created and released to facilitate robust training and generalization across different gripper types and observation conditions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at NVIDIA developed GraspGen, a diffusion-based framework for 6-DOF grasping that incorporates a novel on-generator training method for its discriminator. The system achieved state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks, demonstrating robust generalization across multiple gripper types, observation complexities, and cluttered environments, including an 81.3% success rate in real-world robot experiments.

title: GraspGen: A Diffusion-based Framework for 6-DOF Grasping with On-Generator Training

abstract:
This study introduces a novel language-guided diffusion-based learning
framework, DexTOG, aimed at advancing the field of task-oriented grasping (TOG)
with dexterous hands. Unlike existing methods that mainly focus on 2-finger
grippers, this research addresses the complexities of dexterous manipulation,
where the system must identify non-unique optimal grasp poses under specific
task constraints, cater to multiple valid grasps, and search in a high
degree-of-freedom configuration space in grasp planning. The proposed DexTOG
includes a diffusion-based grasp pose generation model, DexDiffu, and a data
engine to support the DexDiffu. By leveraging DexTOG, we also proposed a new
dataset, DexTOG-80K, which was developed using a shadow robot hand to perform
various tasks on 80 objects from 5 categories, showcasing the dexterity and
multi-tasking capabilities of the robotic hand. This research not only presents
a significant leap in dexterous TOG but also provides a comprehensive dataset
and simulation validation, setting a new benchmark in robotic manipulation
research.

keyInsights: Using a 6D continuous representation for grasp pose rotations significantly enhances stability for diffusion models when adding noise, outperforming quaternion representations.

A data engine that integrates heuristic rules, generative models, and reinforcement learning can effectively mitigate data scarcity for complex dexterous manipulation tasks by generating diverse and validated datasets.
The incorporation of language descriptions enables a single model to generate diverse, task-specific dexterous grasps, allowing robots to understand and execute tasks with intentionality.
originalProblem: Robotic grasping traditionally prioritizes stability with simple grippers, overlooking the specific subsequent manipulation task for dexterous hands.
High-degree-of-freedom dexterous hands lack task-oriented grasping solutions due to complex configuration spaces and multimodal optimal grasp distributions.
There is a lack of large-scale datasets specifically for learning task-oriented dexterous grasps, particularly involving articulated objects.
results: DiffuTOG achieved superior grasp stability with a Q1 score of 0.1067 on seen objects, outperforming state-of-the-art task-agnostic methods like UniDexGrasp (0.0734).

Grasps generated by DexTOG led to significantly higher task success rates in simulation, for instance, 67% for stapler clicking on seen objects compared to a 10% success rate for a GraspTTA* baseline.
A test-time collision handling step substantially reduced object penetration, increasing the percentage of collision-free grasps from 6% to 63% on seen objects, improving practical applicability.
solution: The paper proposes DiffuTOG, a conditional diffusion model that predicts dexterous grasp poses based on object point clouds, a robot hand model, and natural language task descriptions.
An autonomous data generation engine, DexTOG, was developed, employing a coarse-to-fine, sparse-to-dense approach that includes rule-based filtering, DiffuTOG bootstrapping, and RL-based validation.
The researchers generated DexTOG-80K, a dataset comprising 80,000 task-oriented and task-agnostic dexterous grasps for 80 articulated objects across five categories and five tasks.
paper_summary.summary: A language-guided framework named DexTOG introduces DiffuTOG, a diffusion model for predicting task-oriented dexterous grasps, and DexTOG-80K, a large-scale dataset generated by a novel data engine. The framework enables robots to achieve higher task success rates, such as 67% for stapler clicking on seen objects, compared to 10% for a baseline.

title: DexTOG: Learning Task-Oriented Dexterous Grasp with Language

abstract: Grasping in cluttered scenes remains highly challenging for dexterous hands due to the scarcity of data. To address this problem, we present a large-scale synthetic benchmark, encompassing 1319 objects, 8270 scenes, and 427 million grasps. Beyond benchmarking, we also propose a novel two-stage grasping method that learns efficiently from data by using a diffusion model that conditions on local geometry. Our proposed generative method outperforms all baselines in simulation experiments. Furthermore, with the aid of test-time-depth restoration, our method demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, attaining 90.7% real-world dexterous grasping success rate in cluttered scenes.

keyInsights: Large-scale synthetic datasets can effectively address data scarcity for training complex robotic manipulation systems, especially when combined with realistic scene generation through physics simulation.
Generative models, such as diffusion models, are well-suited for dexterous grasping due to its multi-modal nature, enabling the generation of diverse and valid grasp poses.
Careful design of synthetic data and a robust generative model can enable successful zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for dexterous manipulation tasks in cluttered environments.
originalProblem: Dexterous robotic grasping is complex due to high degrees of freedom, intricate contact dynamics, and the multi-modal nature of valid grasp solutions.
A significant scarcity of large-scale, high-quality real-world datasets for multi-fingered dexterous grasping, particularly in cluttered scenes.
Existing datasets for dexterous grasping are often too small, lack realistic clutter, or rely on simplistic grasp generation methods.
results: The proposed method achieved an 85.8% success rate in simulation on the DexGraspNet 2.0 benchmark, outperforming all baseline methods.
It demonstrated successful zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, achieving a 90.7% success rate on real-world dexterous grasping tasks with diverse objects.
The generative approach exhibited superior data efficiency, achieving 60.8% accuracy when trained on only 1/100th of the available data, compared to GSNet's 26.2%.
solution: Introduction of DexGraspNet 2.0, a large-scale synthetic benchmark with 427 million dexterous grasp annotations across 8,270 cluttered scenes and 1,319 diverse objects.
A two-stage generative grasping method that first proposes promising seed points in a cluttered scene using a graspness prediction network.
A conditional diffusion model then generates diverse and high-quality grasp poses conditioned on local geometric features extracted around the selected seed points.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduce DexGraspNet 2.0, a large-scale benchmark comprising 427 million dexterous grasp annotations for cluttered scenes. They also present a two-stage generative grasping method that achieves robust grasping of diverse objects in cluttered environments, demonstrating successful zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.

title: DexGraspNet 2.0: Learning Generative Dexterous Grasping in Large-scale Synthetic Cluttered Scenes

abstract: Synthesizing human motion has advanced rapidly, yet realistic hand motion and bimanual interaction remain underexplored. Whole-body models often miss the fine-grained cues that drive dexterous behavior, finger articulation, contact timing, and inter-hand coordination, and existing resources lack high-fidelity bimanual sequences that capture nuanced finger dynamics and collaboration. To fill this gap, we present HandX, a unified foundation spanning data, annotation, and evaluation. We consolidate and filter existing datasets for quality, and collect a new motion-capture dataset targeting underrepresented bimanual interactions with detailed finger dynamics. For scalable annotation, we introduce a decoupled strategy that extracts representative motion features, e.g., contact events and finger flexion, and then leverages reasoning from large language models to produce fine-grained, semantically rich descriptions aligned with these features. Building on the resulting data and annotations, we benchmark diffusion and autoregressive models with versatile conditioning modes. Experiments demonstrate high-quality dexterous motion generation, supported by our newly proposed hand-focused metrics. We further observe clear scaling trends: larger models trained on larger, higher-quality datasets produce more semantically coherent bimanual motion. Our dataset is released to support future research.

keyInsights: The HandX dataset features significantly higher contact ratio, duration, and frequency, highlighting its focus on dynamic and comprehensive bimanual interactions.
A two-stage decoupled annotation strategy, combining kinematic feature extraction with LLM reasoning, yields higher-quality textual descriptions than direct video captioning.
Empirical scaling laws indicate that jointly increasing dataset size and model capacity consistently improves text-motion alignment and contact accuracy for generative models.
originalProblem: Lack of high-fidelity, large-scale datasets capturing fine-grained finger articulation, diverse bimanual interactions, and precise contact dynamics.
Insufficient fine-grained semantic annotations for complex bimanual motions due to annotation cost and lack of granularity.
Absence of established evaluation protocols and specialized metrics for assessing specific nuances like finger dexterity and bimanual coordination in generated hand motions.
results: The HandX dataset was established, containing 54.2 hours of high-quality bimanual motion data, 5.9 million frames, and 490,000 fine-grained textual descriptions.
Benchmarking showed that diffusion models trained on HandX exhibited improved R-Precision and contact F1 scores, with a 12-layer model achieving optimal overall contact performance before saturation.

Qualitative results demonstrated the models' ability to synthesize realistic, fine-grained finger articulation and complex inter-hand coordination, enabling versatile generation tasks such as motion in-betweening and hand-reaction synthesis.
solution: Develop the HandX dataset by aggregating existing bimanual motion data and collecting new high-fidelity motion capture sequences, unifying them into a 21-joint skeletal representation.
Propose a two-stage automatic annotation framework that extracts kinematic features from motion data and uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate multi-level, fine-grained textual descriptions.
Benchmark text-conditioned bimanual motion generation using diffusion and autoregressive models, enabling versatile control modes like motion in-betweening and hand-reaction synthesis.
paper_summary.summary: A new foundation, HandX, provides a large-scale dataset, an automatic annotation framework, and evaluation metrics for text-conditioned bimanual hand motion generation. It enables the synthesis of fine-grained, contact-aware motions, demonstrating that increasing data volume and model capacity enhances performance in diffusion models.

title: HandX: Scaling Bimanual Motion and Interaction Generation

abstract:
Autonomous bin picking poses significant challenges to vision-driven robotic
systems given the complexity of the problem, ranging from various sensor
modalities, to highly entangled object layouts, to diverse item properties and
gripper types. Existing methods often address the problem from one perspective.
Diverse items and complex bin scenes require diverse picking strategies
together with advanced reasoning. As such, to build robust and effective
machine-learning algorithms for solving this complex task requires significant
amounts of comprehensive and high quality data. Collecting such data in real
world would be too expensive and time prohibitive and therefore intractable
from a scalability perspective. To tackle this big, diverse data problem, we
take inspiration from the recent rise in the concept of metaverses, and
introduce MetaGraspNet, a large-scale photo-realistic bin picking dataset
constructed via physics-based metaverse synthesis. The proposed dataset
contains 217k RGBD images across 82 different article types, with full
annotations for object detection, amodal perception, keypoint detection,
manipulation order and ambidextrous grasp labels for a parallel-jaw and vacuum
gripper. We also provide a real dataset consisting of over 2.3k fully annotated
high-quality RGBD images, divided into 5 levels of difficulties and an unseen
object set to evaluate different object and layout properties. Finally, we
conduct extensive experiments showing that our proposed vacuum seal model and
synthetic dataset achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes to real
world use-cases.

keyInsights: Physics-based metaverse synthesis can effectively generate large-scale, photorealistic datasets suitable for training robust robotic manipulation policies.
A detailed physics-based vacuum seal model significantly improves the accuracy of vacuum grasp success prediction compared to heuristic methods.
Rich, multi-modal annotations, including amodal segmentation, semantic keypoints, and scene layout, are essential for developing generalizable robotic scene understanding and grasp planning.
originalProblem: Robotic bin picking is difficult due to object diversity, cluttered scenes, and complex physical interactions.
A scarcity of comprehensive, annotated datasets hinders the development of robust robotic manipulation systems.
Models trained in simulation often face a "sim-to-real gap," performing poorly in real-world scenarios.
results: The proposed physics-based vacuum seal model achieved high accuracy in predicting vacuum grasp success in real-world experiments.
Models trained on MetaGraspNet exhibited strong sim-to-real transfer, sometimes outperforming models trained solely on real-world data.
Demonstrated an 85% vacuum grasp success rate on seen objects and 79% on novel objects, alongside competitive object detection (0.71 mAP on real-world scenes).
solution: Developed MetaGraspNet, a large-scale dataset synthesized using physics-based metaverse simulation in NVIDIA Isaac Sim.
Incorporates a novel physics-based vacuum seal model to accurately predict vacuum grasp success.
Provides comprehensive annotations for over 1 million RGBD images, covering multiple grasp types and scene understanding tasks.
paper_summary.summary: MetaGraspNet, a large-scale dataset for robotic bin picking, provides over 1 million photorealistic RGBD images and comprehensive annotations by leveraging physics-based metaverse synthesis. The dataset enables training robust robotic systems with strong sim-to-real transfer capabilities for both parallel-jaw and vacuum grasping.

title: MetaGraspNet: A Large-Scale Benchmark Dataset for Scene-Aware  Ambidextrous Bin Picking via Physics-based Metaverse Synthesis

abstract: Handover between a human and a dexterous robotic hand is a fundamental yet challenging task in human-robot collaboration. It requires handling dynamic environments and a wide variety of objects and demands robust and adaptive grasping strategies. However, progress in developing effective dynamic dexterous grasping methods is limited by the absence of high-quality, real-world human-to-robot handover datasets. Existing datasets primarily focus on grasping static objects or rely on synthesized handover motions, which differ significantly from real-world robot motion patterns, creating a substantial gap in applicability. In this paper, we introduce DexH2R, a comprehensive real-world dataset for human-to-robot handovers, built on a dexterous robotic hand. Our dataset captures a diverse range of interactive objects, dynamic motion patterns, rich visual sensor data, and detailed annotations. Additionally, to ensure natural and human-like dexterous motions, we utilize teleoperation for data collection, enabling the robot's movements to align with human behaviors and habits, which is a crucial characteristic for intelligent humanoid robots. Furthermore, we propose an effective solution, DynamicGrasp, for human-to-robot handover and evaluate various state-of-the-art approaches, including auto-regressive models and diffusion policy methods, providing a thorough comparison and analysis. We believe our benchmark will drive advancements in human-to-robot handover research by offering a high-quality dataset, effective solutions, and comprehensive evaluation metrics.

keyInsights: The high fidelity of teleoperated grasping data in DexH2R allows for effective training of grasp pose generative models, achieving strong performance without extensive pre-training on synthetic datasets.
Integrating 3D point cloud input (Diffusion Policy 3D) provides a more robust and safer approach for dynamic object approaching motions compared to 2D image-based or purely autoregressive methods.

A multi-stage approach combining initial grasp preparation, dynamic trajectory generation, and a final goal pose alignment strategy is crucial for successfully executing complex, safe, and physically plausible dexterous handovers.
originalProblem: A scarcity of high-quality, real-world datasets specifically for dynamic human-to-robot handover involving dexterous robotic hands.
Existing datasets are limited to static grasping, human-to-human interactions, or synthetic data, creating a significant reality gap for complex dexterous robot control.
Developing robust and adaptive grasping strategies for multi-fingered robot hands in dynamic human-robot interaction remains a challenge due to the lack of suitable training and evaluation data.
results: Grasp pose generation models trained exclusively on DexH2R achieved an 89.38% success rate for single-force stability, demonstrating the dataset's quality for learning stable grasps.

The Diffusion Policy 3D (DP3) method achieved the highest safety rate (50.1%) and a 66.3% success rate in the 'Easy Mode' for approaching motion, balancing successful grasping with collision avoidance.
In the more challenging 'Hard Mode', DP3 maintained a competitive 27.1% success rate with a 33.7% safety rate, outperforming other baselines under tighter proximity constraints for goal alignment.
solution: Introduction of DexH2R, the first real-world dataset comprising 4282 dynamic human-to-robot handover trials with 39 participants, 56 objects, and 79,911 successful grasp poses, captured with multi-modal sensors and 3D annotations.

Development of DynamicGrasp, a three-stage solution for human-to-robot handover, encompassing grasp pose preparation, approaching motion generation (using autoregressive or diffusion policies), and goal pose alignment.

Establishment of a comprehensive benchmark on DexH2R to evaluate dynamic grasping approaches across metrics like success rate, safety, penetration depth, and diversity, including unseen objects and subjects.
paper_summary.summary: The DexH2R benchmark provides the first real-world dataset for dynamic human-to-robot object handover using dexterous robotic hands, featuring multi-modal data and detailed 3D annotations. An accompanying three-stage solution demonstrates that a Diffusion Policy 3D approach achieves a balanced performance with a 66.3% success rate and 50.1% safety in complex dynamic grasping scenarios.

title: DexH2R: A Benchmark for Dynamic Dexterous Grasping in Human-to-Robot Handover

abstract: Grasping is a fundamental capability for robots to interact with the physical world. Humans, equipped with two hands, autonomously select appropriate grasp strategies based on the shape, size, and weight of objects, enabling robust grasping and subsequent manipulation. In contrast, current robotic grasping remains limited, particularly in multi-strategy settings. Although substantial efforts have targeted parallel-gripper and single-hand grasping, dexterous grasping for bimanual robots remains underexplored, with data being a primary bottleneck. Achieving physically plausible and geometrically conforming grasps that can withstand external wrenches poses significant challenges. To address these issues, we introduce UltraDexGrasp, a framework for universal dexterous grasping with bimanual robots. The proposed data-generation pipeline integrates optimization-based grasp synthesis with planning-based demonstration generation, yielding high-quality and diverse trajectories across multiple grasp strategies. With this framework, we curate UltraDexGrasp-20M, a large-scale, multi-strategy grasp dataset comprising 20 million frames across 1,000 objects. Based on UltraDexGrasp-20M, we further develop a simple yet effective grasp policy that takes point clouds as input, aggregates scene features via unidirectional attention, and predicts control commands. Trained exclusively on synthetic data, the policy achieves robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer and consistently succeeds on novel objects with varied shapes, sizes, and weights, attaining an average success rate of 81.2% in real-world universal dexterous grasping. To facilitate future research on grasping with bimanual robots, we open-source the data generation pipeline at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Integrating optimization-based grasp synthesis with planning-based demonstration generation effectively creates physically plausible and kinematically feasible bimanual grasp data.
Learning from a sufficiently large and diverse synthetic dataset allows the policy to surpass the performance of the data generation process itself, indicating effective data utilization.

Probabilistic action modeling with bounded Gaussian distributions and unidirectional attention in the transformer significantly improves policy robustness and learning efficiency for complex dexterous tasks.
originalProblem: A severe scarcity of high-quality, diverse data exists for training bimanual dexterous grasping policies in multi-fingered robots.
Current robotic grasping methods often lack support for multiple strategies and struggle to scale to the complexity of bimanual operations with high degrees of freedom.
Achieving robust sim-to-real transfer for policies trained solely on synthetic data remains a significant challenge, hindering real-world deployment.
results: The UltraDexGrasp policy achieved an 84.0% average success rate in simulation across seen and unseen objects, outperforming baselines like DP3 (46.7%) and DexGraspNet (58.8%).
It demonstrated robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, achieving an 81.2% average success rate on 25 diverse real-world objects (3.6g to 1,095g, 18 cm³ to 26,400 cm³ volume).

The policy generalized effectively to novel objects and successfully adapted multiple grasping strategies (two-finger pinch, three-finger tripod, whole-hand, bimanual) based on object characteristics.
solution: A novel data generation pipeline creates UltraDexGrasp-20M, the first large-scale, multi-strategy dataset with 20 million frames from 1,000 diverse objects, integrating optimization-based grasp synthesis with planning-based bimanual trajectories.
A universal dexterous grasp policy utilizes a PointNet++ encoder and a decoder-only transformer with unidirectional attention to predict control commands directly from scene point clouds.
The policy employs probabilistic action modeling via a bounded Gaussian distribution to predict actions, enhancing training stability and overall performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Laboratory developed UltraDexGrasp, a framework that generates a large-scale synthetic dataset and trains a policy for universal dexterous grasping in bimanual robots. The system achieves an 81.2% success rate in real-world scenarios on diverse, unseen objects through zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.

title: UltraDexGrasp: Learning Universal Dexterous Grasping for Bimanual Robots with Synthetic Data

abstract: Dexterous grasp datasets are vital for embodied intelligence, but mostly emphasize grasp stability, ignoring functional grasps needed for tasks like opening bottle caps or holding cup handles. Most rely on bulky, costly, and hard-to-control high-DOF Shadow Hands. Inspired by the human hand's underactuated mechanism, we establish UniFucGrasp, a universal functional grasp annotation strategy and dataset for multiple dexterous hand types. Based on biomimicry, it maps natural human motions to diverse hand structures and uses geometry-based force closure to ensure functional, stable, human-like grasps. This method supports low-cost, efficient collection of diverse, high-quality functional grasps. Finally, we establish the first multi-hand functional grasp dataset and provide a synthesis model to validate its effectiveness. Experiments on the UFG dataset, IsaacSim, and complex robotic tasks show that our method improves functional manipulation accuracy and grasp stability, demonstrates improved adaptability across multiple robotic hands, helping to alleviate annotation cost and generalization challenges in dexterous grasping. The project page is at this https URL.

keyInsights: Human-hand-inspired motion priors, when mapped through a unified strategy, can effectively generalize functional grasp annotations across structurally diverse robotic hands.

Conditioning grasp generation models on rich hand point cloud information significantly enhances the accuracy and functionality of generated grasps compared to simpler identity encodings or no conditioning.
Integrating geometry-based force closure analysis within the annotation and post-processing stages is crucial for ensuring the physical stability of functionally-driven robotic grasps.
originalProblem: Existing research largely focuses on grasp stability, overlooking the crucial functional aspects of grasping required for task-specific manipulation.
Current large-scale functional grasp datasets are primarily hardware-dependent, tailored for single, often expensive robotic hands, which limits generalization and accessibility.

Prior functional grasp annotation methods were inefficient, lacked real pose supervision, or did not systematically evaluate grasp stability, leading to a scarcity of comprehensive functional grasp data.
results: The UniFucGrasp dataset comprises over 100,000 high-quality, stable functional grasp annotations for 1,108 objects, supporting both fully-actuated (ShadowHand) and underactuated (InspireHand, HnuHand) dexterous hands.

In simulation, the functional gesture generation model achieved a 69.55% grasp success rate, outperforming the state-of-the-art DFG dataset (65.52%) with specific improvements for challenging objects like mugs and drills.

Real-world experiments demonstrated improved grasping success rates by 11.54% for InspireHand and 23.08% for HnuHand over DFG, and significantly (over 75%) enhanced functional task performance across five unseen object categories.
solution: Developed a human-hand-inspired unified annotation strategy that maps human hand motions to diverse robotic hand structures (fully-actuated and underactuated) using sparse matrix optimization (JAM).

Created UniFucGrasp, the first large-scale, multi-hand functional grasp dataset, featuring over 100,000 high-quality annotations for 1,108 objects, with grasp stability validated by force-closure analysis.
Proposed a functional gesture generation model using a conditional VAE with DGCNN and Transformer architecture to synthesize unified functional grasps conditioned on object and hand point clouds.
paper_summary.summary: UniFucGrasp presents a human-hand-inspired, unified functional grasp annotation strategy and the first large-scale, multi-hand dataset for diverse dexterous robotic hands. This framework enables the generation of more accurate and stable functional grasps, showing increased success rates for complex tasks across various robotic platforms in both simulation and real-world experiments.

title: UniFucGrasp: Human-Hand-Inspired Unified Functional Grasp Annotation Strategy and Dataset for Diverse Dexterous Hands

abstract: Grasping is fundamental to robotic manipulation, and recent advances in large-scale grasping datasets have provided essential training data and evaluation benchmarks, accelerating the development of learning-based methods for robust object grasping. However, most existing datasets exclude deformable bodies due to the lack of scalable, robust simulation pipelines, limiting the development of generalizable models for compliant grippers and soft manipulands. To address these challenges, we present GRIP, a General Robotic Incremental Potential contact simulation dataset for universal grasping. GRIP leverages an optimized Incremental Potential Contact (IPC)-based simulator for multi-environment data generation, achieving up to 48x speedup while ensuring efficient, intersection- and inversion-free simulations for compliant grippers and deformable objects. Our fully automated pipeline generates and evaluates diverse grasp interactions across 1,200 objects and 100,000 grasp poses, incorporating both soft and rigid grippers. The GRIP dataset enables applications such as neural grasp generation and stress field prediction.
title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

abstract: Dual-arm robotic grasping is crucial for handling large objects that require stable and coordinated manipulation. While single-arm grasping has been extensively studied, datasets tailored for dual-arm settings remain scarce. We introduce a large-scale dataset of 16 million dual-arm grasps, evaluated under improved force-closure constraints. Additionally, we develop a benchmark dataset containing 300 objects with approximately 30,000 grasps, evaluated in a physics simulation environment, providing a better grasp quality assessment for dual-arm grasp synthesis methods. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our dataset by training a Dual-Arm Grasp Classifier network that outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by 15\%, achieving higher grasp success rates and improved generalization across objects.
title: DG16M: A Large-Scale Dataset for Dual-Arm Grasping with Force-Optimized Grasps

abstract:
This paper explores a novel task "Dexterous Grasp as You Say" (DexGYS),
enabling robots to perform dexterous grasping based on human commands expressed
in natural language. However, the development of this field is hindered by the
lack of datasets with natural human guidance; thus, we propose a
language-guided dexterous grasp dataset, named DexGYSNet, offering high-quality
dexterous grasp annotations along with flexible and fine-grained human language
guidance. Our dataset construction is cost-efficient, with the carefully-design
hand-object interaction retargeting strategy, and the LLM-assisted language
guidance annotation system. Equipped with this dataset, we introduce the
DexGYSGrasp framework for generating dexterous grasps based on human language
instructions, with the capability of producing grasps that are intent-aligned,
high quality and diversity. To achieve this capability, our framework
decomposes the complex learning process into two manageable progressive
objectives and introduce two components to realize them. The first component
learns the grasp distribution focusing on intention alignment and generation
diversity. And the second component refines the grasp quality while maintaining
intention consistency. Extensive experiments are conducted on DexGYSNet and
real world environments for validation.

keyInsights: Object penetration loss, while crucial for grasp quality, significantly hinders a single model's ability to learn diverse and intention-aligned dexterous grasp distributions.
Decomposing the learning process into separate generative (intention/diversity) and regressive (quality refinement) objectives effectively resolves the conflict posed by penetration loss.
Large-scale, language-guided dexterous grasp datasets can be created cost-effectively by combining human-to-robot grasp retargeting with LLM-assisted natural language annotation.
originalProblem: The absence of large-scale, high-quality datasets for language-guided dexterous grasping hinders the development of data-driven methods.

Generating dexterous grasps that are simultaneously aligned with human intent, high-quality, and diverse is challenging due to conflicting learning objectives, especially the trade-off with penetration loss.
results: The DexGYSGrasp framework surpassed prior state-of-the-art methods in language-guided dexterous grasping, achieving superior intention alignment and grasp diversity.
It produced comparable grasp quality (evaluated in Isaac Gym simulation) and acceptable penetration depth while excelling in intention and diversity.

Real-world experiments demonstrated the framework's practical applicability, successfully generating and executing intention-aligned dexterous grasps on physical objects using an Allegro hand based on natural language commands.
solution: The DexGYSNet dataset was constructed using Hand-Object Interaction Retargeting (HOIR) for efficient human-to-robot grasp transfer and LLM-assisted annotation for fine-grained language guidance.
The DexGYSGrasp framework employs a progressive learning strategy, decoupling the generation of intention-aligned and diverse grasps from the refinement of grasp quality.

It uses an Intention and Diversity Grasp Component (IDGC) (a conditional diffusion model without penetration loss) for broad distribution learning, followed by a Quality Grasp Component (QGC) for physical feasibility.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sun Yat-sen University and Stanford University developed DexGYSGrasp, a framework enabling natural language-guided dexterous grasping for multi-fingered robots, alongside DexGYSNet, a large-scale, cost-effective dataset for this task. The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in intention alignment and grasp diversity, with comparable quality, compared to prior methods.

title: Grasp as You Say: Language-guided Dexterous Grasp Generation

abstract: Robotic dexterous grasping is important for interacting with the environment. To unleash the potential of data-driven models for dexterous grasping, a large-scale, high-quality dataset is essential. While gradient-based optimization offers a promising way for constructing such datasets, previous works suffer from limitations, such as inefficiency, strong assumptions in the grasp quality energy, or limited object sets for experiments. Moreover, the lack of a standard benchmark for comparing different methods and datasets hinders progress in this field. To address these challenges, we develop a highly efficient synthesis system and a comprehensive benchmark with MuJoCo for dexterous grasping. We formulate grasp synthesis as a bilevel optimization problem, combining a novel lower-level quadratic programming (QP) with an upper-level gradient descent process. By leveraging recent advances in CUDA-accelerated robotic libraries and GPU-based QP solvers, our system can parallelize thousands of grasps and synthesize over 49 grasps per second on a single 3090 GPU. Our synthesized grasps for Shadow, Allegro, and Leap hands all achieve a success rate above 75% in simulation, with a penetration depth under 1 mm, outperforming existing baselines on nearly all metrics. Compared to the previous large-scale dataset, DexGraspNet, our dataset significantly improves the performance of learning models, with a success rate from around 40% to 80% in simulation. Real-world testing of the trained model on the Shadow Hand achieves an 81% success rate across 20 diverse objects. The codes and datasets are released on our project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Bilevel optimization effectively determines optimal contact forces under realistic friction cone constraints, enhancing the physical accuracy and stability of generated grasps.

Aggressive GPU parallelization across different optimization stages (upper-level hand pose and lower-level contact force QP) is essential for achieving the scale needed for data-driven dexterous grasping.
The quality of the generated grasp dataset is critical for downstream learning models, with higher-quality data leading to dramatic performance improvements in robotic manipulation tasks.

Generating practical pre-grasp and squeeze poses alongside final grasp poses is crucial for facilitating collision-free arm trajectories and effective force application in real-world robot deployment.
originalProblem: High dimensionality of dexterous hands makes direct sampling for grasp poses inefficient and existing gradient-based methods too slow for large-scale data generation.
Previous analytical grasp synthesis methods rely on unrealistic assumptions about contact forces or limited object sets, leading to suboptimal grasp quality.
The lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets hinders the development of robust learning-based models for dexterous grasping.
Absence of a standardized benchmark prevents fair comparison and slows down progress across different dexterous grasp synthesis methods.
results: BODex achieved a synthesis speed of over 49 grasps per second on a single GPU, representing a 50x speedup compared to existing baselines.
The system generated grasps with a 79.9% simulation success rate (Allegro Hand) and significantly improved physical soundness (e.g., 0.51 mm penetration depth vs. 4.49 mm for DexGraspNet).

Learning models trained on the BODex dataset showed substantial performance gains, with UniDexGrasp achieving an 80.1% simulation success rate, a significant increase from 42.6% when trained on prior datasets.
The approach demonstrated strong real-world applicability with an 81% success rate on a Shadow Hand grasping 20 diverse objects, validated by physical experiments.
solution: Introduces BODex, a bilevel optimization framework that frames grasp synthesis as an upper-level hand pose optimization and a lower-level quadratic programming (QP) for optimal contact forces.
Leverages extensive GPU acceleration using cuRobo and a batched ReLU-QP solver to parallelize computations, achieving high synthesis speeds.
Employs a coarse-to-fine contact modeling strategy, combining sphere-based approximations for initial exploration with mesh-based GJK algorithm for precise contact refinement.
Establishes a comprehensive and reproducible benchmark using the MuJoCo simulator, supporting popular dexterous hands and standardized evaluation metrics.
paper_summary.summary: BODex presents a GPU-accelerated bilevel optimization framework for synthesizing high-quality, large-scale dexterous grasps, achieving over 49 grasps per second and a 50x speedup over prior methods. This approach enables learning models trained on its dataset to reach an 80.1% simulation success rate and an 81% real-world success rate on a Shadow Hand.

title: BODex: Scalable and Efficient Robotic Dexterous Grasp Synthesis Using Bilevel Optimization

abstract: Data scarcity fundamentally limits the generalization of bimanual dexterous manipulation, as real-world data collection for dexterous hands is expensive and labor-intensive. Human manipulation videos, as a direct carrier of manipulation knowledge, offer significant potential for scaling up robot learning. However, the substantial embodiment gap between human hands and robotic dexterous hands makes direct pretraining from human videos extremely challenging. To bridge this gap and unleash the potential of large-scale human manipulation video data, we propose DexImit, an automated framework that converts monocular human manipulation videos into physically plausible robot data, without any additional information. DexImit employs a four-stage generation pipeline: (1) reconstructing hand-object interactions from arbitrary viewpoints with near-metric scale; (2) performing subtask decomposition and bimanual scheduling; (3) synthesizing robot trajectories consistent with the demonstrated interactions; (4) comprehensive data augmentation for zero-shot real-world deployment. Building on these designs, DexImit can generate large-scale robot data based on human videos, either from the Internet or video generation models. DexImit is capable of handling diverse manipulation tasks, including tool use (e.g., cutting an apple), long-horizon tasks (e.g., making a beverage), and fine-grained manipulations (e.g., stacking cups).
