organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Near-metric scale reconstruction of hand-object interactions from monocular videos is achievable by leveraging human hand size as a consistent scale prior.
Decomposing long-horizon bimanual tasks into subtasks with an action-centric scheduling algorithm effectively manages complex coordination and reduces error accumulation.
Force-closure based grasp synthesis, combined with keyframe motion planning and robust 6D object pose tracking, yields physically plausible robot trajectories for intricate manipulations.
Strategic data augmentation, including scale randomization that adjusts finger articulation instead of re-generating grasps, is crucial for successful zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
originalProblem: Collecting large-scale datasets for bimanual dexterous hands is expensive and labor-intensive, leading to data scarcity.
Direct use of human videos for robot learning suffers from a significant "embodiment gap" between human and robotic hands.
Prior 3D reconstruction methods from videos often require precise depth information or perfect reconstruction accuracy, limiting their scalability and robustness for complex, long-horizon tasks.
results: Achieved an 82% success rate in 4D object trajectory reconstruction on 100 short-horizon tasks using a combination of SpatialTracker v2 and FoundationPose++.
Policies trained on DexImit data consistently outperformed baseline methods in simulation, achieving high success rates across six tasks, including 100% for 'Put Cup', 'Grapefruit', and 'Fruits'.

Enabled successful zero-shot real-world deployment on bimanual robotic systems, with ablation studies showing that object scale and visual observation augmentations were critical for robust sim-to-real transfer.
solution: A four-stage pipeline performs 4D hand-object trajectory reconstruction from monocular human videos using hand-size-based scale approximation and robust 6D pose estimation.
An action-centric scheduling algorithm decomposes and coordinates bimanual subtasks, followed by structured action generation via force-closure-based grasp synthesis and keyframe motion planning.
Comprehensive data augmentation, including object pose, scale, camera, and observation noise, enhances policy robustness and sim-to-real transfer.
paper_summary.summary: DexImit is an automated framework that generates physically plausible bimanual dexterous manipulation data for robots directly from monocular human videos, overcoming data scarcity without requiring depth information. It achieves high success rates in simulation and facilitates zero-shot real-world deployment for complex tasks through robust data generation and comprehensive augmentation.

title: DexImit: Learning Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation from Monocular Human Videos

abstract: Dexterous manipulation with a multi-finger hand is one of the most challenging problems in robotics. While recent progress in imitation learning has largely improved the sample efficiency compared to Reinforcement Learning, the learned policy can hardly generalize to manipulate novel objects, given limited expert demonstrations. In this paper, we propose to learn dexterous manipulation using large-scale demonstrations with diverse 3D objects in a category, which are generated from a human grasp affordance model. This generalizes the policy to novel object instances within the same category. To train the policy, we propose a novel imitation learning objective jointly with a geometric representation learning objective using our demonstrations. By experimenting with relocating diverse objects in simulation, we show that our approach outperforms baselines with a large margin when manipulating novel objects. We also ablate the importance on 3D object representation learning for manipulation. We include videos, code, and additional information on the project website - this https URL .

keyInsights: Generating large-scale, diverse, and human-like demonstrations synthetically from grasp affordance models is a scalable solution to the data scarcity problem in dexterous manipulation.
Dynamically weighting demonstrations based on the policy's current performance and approximating true advantage values improves learning efficiency and effectiveness from diverse datasets.
Jointly training the 3D geometric representation encoder (PointNet) via behavior cloning with a slower update schedule promotes stability and enables robust generalization to novel object geometries.
originalProblem: Dexterous manipulation with high-DOF multi-finger robot hands is challenging, requiring complex control and planning.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) for dexterous manipulation suffers from high sample inefficiency and poor generalization to novel objects or environments.
Imitation Learning (IL) is bottlenecked by the scarcity, cost, and limited diversity of high-quality expert demonstrations, which hinders the learning of generalizable policies.
results: ILAD achieved an average success rate of 96% on unseen object instances across five categories, substantially outperforming `DAPG+PC` (64%) and pure `RL` baselines (11-16%).

Policies trained with large-scale synthetically generated demonstrations (1000 trajectories per category) consistently demonstrated superior generalization compared to smaller datasets (100 trajectories).

Ablation studies confirmed that both the novel imitation learning objective enhancements (dynamic weighting and improved advantage approximation) and the joint geometric representation learning strategy were critical for ILAD's high performance.
solution: A two-stage pipeline, `ILAD`, is proposed to learn generalizable dexterous manipulation from diverse object interactions.
Stage 1 automates large-scale, human-like demonstration generation by using a pre-trained human grasp affordance model (`GraspCVAE`) and Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) for motion planning.

Stage 2 employs an enhanced imitation learning objective with dynamic likelihood weights and improved advantage approximation, combined with a joint learning strategy for a 3D PointNet encoder, to train a robust policy.
paper_summary.summary: ILAD (Imitation Learning from Affordance Demonstration) is a framework that enables multi-finger robot hands to learn generalizable dexterous manipulation skills for novel objects. It leverages large-scale, synthetically generated human grasp demonstrations and an enhanced imitation learning objective with joint geometric representation learning, achieving an average success rate of 96% on unseen object instances.

title: Learning Generalizable Dexterous Manipulation from Human Grasp Affordance

abstract: Functional grasping with dexterous robotic hands is a key capability for enabling tool use and complex manipulation, yet progress has been constrained by two persistent bottlenecks: the scarcity of large-scale datasets and the absence of integrated semantic and geometric reasoning in learned models. In this work, we present CorDex, a framework that robustly learns dexterous functional grasps of novel objects from synthetic data generated from just a single human demonstration. At the core of our approach is a correspondence-based data engine that generates diverse, high-quality training data in simulation. Based on the human demonstration, our data engine generates diverse object instances of the same category, transfers the expert grasp to the generated objects through correspondence estimation, and adapts the grasp through optimization. Building on the generated data, we introduce a multimodal prediction network that integrates visual and geometric information. By devising a local-global fusion module and an importance-aware sampling mechanism, we enable robust and computationally efficient prediction of functional dexterous grasps. Through extensive experiments across various object categories, we demonstrate that CorDex generalizes well to unseen object instances and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

keyInsights: Leveraging large-scale pre-trained 2D image matching is more robust for transferring grasp knowledge across diverse object instances than unreliable 3D correspondence methods.
Fusing both visual (semantic) information from RGB images and geometric properties from depth data is crucial for learning truly functional grasps beyond mere stability.
An autonomous, scalable data generation engine can effectively overcome the data scarcity bottleneck in dexterous manipulation by synthesizing high-quality training examples from minimal human input.
originalProblem: Scarcity of large-scale, high-quality datasets for learning dexterous functional grasping, which are costly and labor-intensive to acquire.
Difficulty integrating both semantic (task-relevant) and geometric (stability) reasoning into robotic grasping models, limiting functional appropriateness.
Limited generalization of existing one-shot grasp transfer methods to novel object instances due to significant appearance and shape variations.
results: The data engine successfully generated a dataset of 900 diverse objects and 1.08 million RGB-D images (approximately 11 million image–grasp pairs) for nine functional categories.
Achieved an 88.5% average success rate for the Shadow Hand and 74.7% for the Inspire Hand in simulation across nine tasks, substantially outperforming existing baselines.
Demonstrated an average success rate of 69% on unseen real-world objects, validating robust sim-to-real transfer and practical applicability with efficient inference times (0.36-0.92 seconds).
solution: A correspondence-based data engine generates millions of diverse 3D object models and functional grasps from a single human demonstration by leveraging robust 2D image matching and physics-informed grasp adaptation.
A multimodal prediction network integrates RGB images for semantic cues and depth data for geometric properties, using importance-aware sampling and local-global fusion modules.
The system predicts a dense distance matrix between robot hand and object points, enabling recovery of a grasp that is both stable and functionally appropriate.
paper_summary.summary: CorDex is a framework that learns functional dexterous grasping for new objects from a single human video demonstration by autonomously generating diverse synthetic data and utilizing a multimodal prediction network. It achieved an 88.5% average success rate in simulation and 69% on unseen real-world objects, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

title: Generate, Transfer, Adapt: Learning Functional Dexterous Grasping from a Single Human Demonstration

abstract: Robotic grasping is one of the most fundamental tasks in robotic manipulation, and grasp detection/generation has long been the subject of extensive research. Recently, language-driven grasp generation has emerged as a promising direction due to its practical interaction capabilities. However, most existing approaches either lack sufficient reasoning and generalization capabilities or depend on complex modular pipelines. Moreover, current grasp foundation models tend to overemphasize dialog and object semantics, resulting in inferior performance and restriction to single-object grasping. To maintain strong reasoning ability and generalization in cluttered environments, we propose VCoT-Grasp, an end-to-end grasp foundation model that incorporates visual chain-of-thought reasoning to enhance visual understanding for grasp generation. VCoT-Grasp adopts a multi-turn processing paradigm that dynamically focuses on visual inputs while providing interpretable reasoning traces. For training, we refine and introduce a large-scale dataset, VCoT-GraspSet, comprising 167K synthetic images with over 1.36M grasps, as well as 400+ real-world images with more than 1.2K grasps, annotated with intermediate bounding boxes. Extensive experiments on both VCoT-GraspSet and real robot demonstrate that our method significantly improves grasp success rates and generalizes effectively to unseen objects, backgrounds, and distractors. More details can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: Explicitly incorporating intermediate visual reasoning steps, like dynamic object localization and zooming, substantially improves grasp prediction accuracy and generalization compared to direct end-to-end processing.
Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) possess inherent spatial priors, and their pre-trained location tokens can be effectively repurposed for fine-grained spatial tasks like robotic grasping.

High-quality, large-scale datasets with crucial intermediate visual annotations are essential for effectively training foundation models that leverage multi-turn visual reasoning in robotic manipulation.
originalProblem: Existing language-driven grasping methods lack explicit reasoning, struggling with generalization to unseen objects and cluttered scenes.
Prior grasp foundation models, such as RT-Grasp, overemphasize textual reasoning, leading to suboptimal visual understanding and poor generalization.
Robotic grasp models often process images at a fixed granularity, limiting their flexibility and efficiency in capturing fine details crucial for precise grasp generation.
results: VCoT-Grasp achieved state-of-the-art performance on synthetic data, with an 83.60% success rate on seen objects and 58.98% on unseen objects, outperforming RT-Grasp (58.93% seen, 44.79% unseen).
In real-world tests using a Kinova Gen3 robot, the model achieved 0.76 average success on seen objects and 0.71 on unseen objects, consistently surpassing GR-ConvNet+CLIP and RT-Grasp.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of VCoT reasoning, demonstrating a significant performance drop without it, and highlighted that using pre-trained VLM location tokens for action decoding was more effective than MLP or diffusion heads.
solution: VCoT-Grasp proposes an end-to-end foundation model built on the PaliGemma2-3B VLM, integrating an image encoder, visual projector, LLM backbone, and an action head.

It implements Visual Chain-of-Thought (VCoT) reasoning, a multi-turn process where the VLM first localizes the target object with a bounding box, then crops and processes a zoomed-in image for fine-grained grasp generation.
A refined VCoT-GraspSet dataset was curated, comprising 167K synthetic and 400+ real-world images with intermediate bounding box annotations, to facilitate the training of the VCoT reasoning steps.
paper_summary.summary: VCoT-Grasp introduces a foundation model for language-driven grasp generation that employs visual chain-of-thought reasoning to improve object localization and grasp precision. The model achieves enhanced generalization to unseen objects, reaching a 58.98% success rate on out-of-distribution synthetic data and 71% in real-world scenarios, surpassing prior methods.

title: VCoT-Grasp: Grasp Foundation Models with Visual Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Language-driven Grasp Generation

abstract: Dexterous grasp generation aims to produce grasp poses that align with task requirements and human interpretable grasp semantics. However, achieving semantically controllable dexterous grasp synthesis remains highly challenging due to the lack of unified modeling of multiple semantic dimensions, including grasp taxonomy, contact semantics, and functional affordance. To address these limitations, we present OmniDexVLG, a multimodal, semantics aware grasp generation framework capable of producing structurally diverse and semantically coherent dexterous grasps under joint language and visual guidance. Our approach begins with OmniDexDataGen, a semantic rich dexterous grasp dataset generation pipeline that integrates grasp taxonomy guided configuration sampling, functional affordance contact point sampling, taxonomy aware differential force closure grasp sampling, and physics based optimization and validation, enabling systematic coverage of diverse grasp types. We further introduce OmniDexReasoner, a multimodal grasp type semantic reasoning module that leverages multi agent collaboration, retrieval augmented generation, and chain of thought reasoning to infer grasp related semantics and generate high quality annotations that align language instructions with task specific grasp intent. Building upon these components, we develop a unified Vision Language Grasping generation model that explicitly incorporates grasp taxonomy, contact structure, and functional affordance semantics, enabling fine grained control over grasp synthesis from natural language instructions. Extensive experiments in simulation and real world object grasping and ablation studies demonstrate that our method substantially outperforms state of the art approaches in terms of grasp diversity, contact semantic diversity, functional affordance diversity, and semantic consistency.

keyInsights: Generating diverse and semantically rich grasp datasets requires systematic sampling that considers grasp taxonomy, functional affordances, and contact semantics from the outset.

Augmenting LMMs with retrieval-augmented generation and chain-of-thought prompting is effective for grounding their semantic understanding in complex physical interactions and mitigating hallucinations.

A unified framework that explicitly models and leverages multi-dimensional semantics (taxonomy, contact, affordance) across all stages of dexterous grasp generation enables fine-grained control and improved robot manipulation.
originalProblem: Existing dexterous grasp generation lacks fine-grained semantic control, such as specific grasp taxonomy or contact types, beyond basic functional grasping.
Current dexterous grasp datasets suffer from limited diversity and semantic richness, hindering the training of models capable of human-like manipulation.
Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) often produce "semantic hallucinations" when reasoning about complex physical hand-object interactions for dexterous grasping.
results: OmniDexDataGen generated highly diverse grasps, achieving a KL divergence of 0.047 for grasp type diversity and 0.055 for affordance diversity, indicating superior coverage over baselines.

OmniDexReasoner, utilizing Chain-of-Thought and Retrieval-Augmented Generation, improved grasp taxonomy accuracy to 0.97 (coarse) and 0.83 (fine-grained) with GPT-4o, effectively mitigating LMM hallucinations.

OmniDexGraspNet achieved a grasp success rate of 0.80 in simulation and 0.71 in real-world experiments, outperforming GraspGPT, while also demonstrating higher semantic consistency across multiple dimensions.
solution: OmniDexDataGen creates a large, semantically rich dataset of dexterous grasps by systematically sampling diverse grasp configurations and validating them with differential force-closure and physics simulation.

OmniDexReasoner leverages LMMs, enhanced with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, to achieve robust multi-dimensional semantic understanding (taxonomy, contact, affordance) of grasps.

OmniDexGraspNet is a diffusion-based vision-language model that generates dexterous grasp poses, conditioned on partial object point clouds and the detailed semantic descriptions derived from the LMM-guided reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: OmniDexVLG introduces a framework that integrates multi-dimensional semantic understanding, including grasp taxonomy, contact semantics, and functional affordance, into dexterous grasp generation for robots. This approach achieves higher grasp success rates and improved semantic consistency in both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

title: OmniDexVLG: Learning Dexterous Grasp Generation from Vision Language Model-Guided Grasp Semantics, Taxonomy and Functional Affordance

abstract: Foundation models such as ChatGPT have made significant strides in robotic tasks due to their universal representation of real-world domains. In this paper, we leverage foundation models to tackle grasp detection, a persistent challenge in robotics with broad industrial applications. Despite numerous grasp datasets, their object diversity remains limited compared to real-world figures. Fortunately, foundation models possess an extensive repository of real-world knowledge, including objects we encounter in our daily lives. As a consequence, a promising solution to the limited representation in previous grasp datasets is to harness the universal knowledge embedded in these foundation models. We present Grasp-Anything, a new large-scale grasp dataset synthesized from foundation models to implement this solution. Grasp-Anything excels in diversity and magnitude, boasting 1M samples with text descriptions and more than 3M objects, surpassing prior datasets. Empirically, we show that Grasp-Anything successfully facilitates zero-shot grasp detection on vision-based tasks and real-world robotic experiments. Our dataset and code are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Hanoi University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Foundation models offer a scalable method to synthesize high-quality, diverse, and language-driven robotic data, mitigating the limitations of traditional, manually collected datasets.
A data-centric strategy, emphasizing comprehensive and varied training data, is vital for achieving robust generalization and practical performance in robotic grasp detection.
Including natural language descriptions in grasp datasets opens avenues for advanced human-robot interaction and enables robots to understand and execute tasks based on linguistic input.
originalProblem: Existing grasp datasets suffer from limited object diversity and unrealistic scene configurations, impeding generalization of robotic grasping models to real-world scenarios.
Most datasets lack natural language descriptions for scene arrangements, limiting their application in language-driven human-robot interaction.
Prior research has shown "model-centric" approaches for grasp detection often yield unstable results, highlighting a need for improved training data.
results: The Grasp-Anything dataset contains 1 million samples and over 3 million objects, spanning 236 distinct LVIS categories, representing a substantial increase in scale and diversity compared to prior datasets.

Robotic experiments showed that models trained on Grasp-Anything achieved higher grasp success rates on a KUKA robot: 93.3% for single-object and 91.6% for cluttered scenes, outperforming models trained on alternative datasets.

Grasp-Anything consistently enhanced zero-shot grasp detection and cross-dataset transfer learning, improving performance across various deep learning models and achieving up to a 29% increase in transfer learning success rates.
solution: Utilized a large language model (ChatGPT) with prompt engineering and context augmentation to generate 1 million diverse scene descriptions containing multiple graspable objects.
Synthesized images from these descriptions using a text-to-image foundation model (Stable Diffusion 2.1), followed by visual grounding and instance segmentation to extract object masks.
Annotated 2D grasp poses for identified objects using a pre-trained model and refined them based on a modified grasp quality metric derived from object segmentation masks.
paper_summary.summary: Grasp-Anything is a large-scale, language-driven dataset of 1 million samples and over 3 million objects, synthesized using foundation models to enhance generalization in robotic grasp detection. The dataset enables models to achieve a 93.3% grasp success rate for single objects and 91.6% for cluttered scenes on a KUKA robot, outperforming models trained on existing datasets.

title: Grasp-Anything: Large-scale Grasp Dataset from Foundation Models

abstract: Grasp detection is a persistent and intricate challenge with various industrial applications. Recently, many methods and datasets have been proposed to tackle the grasp detection problem. However, most of them do not consider using natural language as a condition to detect the grasp poses. In this paper, we introduce Grasp-Anything++, a new language-driven grasp detection dataset featuring 1M samples, over 3M objects, and upwards of 10M grasping instructions. We utilize foundation models to create a large-scale scene corpus with corresponding images and grasp prompts. We approach the language-driven grasp detection task as a conditional generation problem. Drawing on the success of diffusion models in generative tasks and given that language plays a vital role in this task, we propose a new language-driven grasp detection method based on diffusion models. Our key contribution is the contrastive training objective, which explicitly contributes to the denoising process to detect the grasp pose given the language instructions. We illustrate that our approach is theoretically supportive. The intensive experiments show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches and allows real-world robotic grasping. Finally, we demonstrate our large-scale dataset enables zero-short grasp detection and is a challenging benchmark for future work. Project website: this https URL
title: Language-driven Grasp Detection

abstract: Robotic dexterous grasping is the first step to enable human-like dexterous object manipulation and thus a crucial robotic technology. However, dexterous grasping is much more under-explored than object grasping with parallel grippers, partially due to the lack of a large-scale dataset. In this work, we present a large-scale robotic dexterous grasp dataset, DexGraspNet, generated by our proposed highly efficient synthesis method that can be generally applied to any dexterous hand. Our method leverages a deeply accelerated differentiable force closure estimator and thus can efficiently and robustly synthesize stable and diverse grasps on a large scale. We choose ShadowHand and generate 1.32 million grasps for 5355 objects, covering more than 133 object categories and containing more than 200 diverse grasps for each object instance, with all grasps having been validated by the Isaac Gym simulator. Compared to the previous dataset from Liu et al. generated by GraspIt!, our dataset has not only more objects and grasps, but also higher diversity and quality. Via performing cross-dataset experiments, we show that training several algorithms of dexterous grasp synthesis on our dataset significantly outperforms training on the previous one. To access our data and code, including code for human and Allegro grasp synthesis, please visit our project page: this https URL.

organization_info.name: Beijing Institute for General Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Refinements in grasp initialization and energy function formulation are crucial for enhancing the efficiency, success rate, and diversity of differentiable grasp synthesis.
Optimizing underlying computational components and simplifying optimization algorithms can lead to substantial speedups, making large-scale synthetic dataset generation feasible.

A sufficiently large and diverse dataset of physically validated grasps is essential for significantly advancing the performance and generalization capabilities of learning-based dexterous grasping algorithms.
originalProblem: Dexterous robotic hands, despite their potential for complex manipulation, are under-explored due to the lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets for training.
Existing datasets for dexterous grasping are small, exhibit limited diversity, and often produce unnatural or stiff grasps.
Previous simulation-based grasp generation techniques suffered from low success rates, slow convergence, and sensitivity to object mesh quality, hindering large-scale data creation.
results: Created DexGraspNet, a dataset comprising 1.32 million physically validated dexterous grasps for 5355 unique objects, making it two orders of magnitude larger than prior datasets.
Achieved a 50x speedup in grasp generation efficiency, allowing the full dataset to be generated in 950 GPU hours.

Models trained on DexGraspNet consistently demonstrated improved simulation success rates (e.g., 67.5% for DDG) and higher grasp diversity (increased joint angle entropy) compared to those trained on previous datasets.
solution: Improved an existing differentiable grasp synthesis method by introducing a robust hand pose initialization, a reverse penetration energy calculation, and additional energy terms for self-penetration and joint limits.
Streamlined the optimization process to simple gradient descent and utilized a modified Kaolin for efficient signed distance computations, leading to a significant speedup.
Implemented a rigorous physical validation pipeline using the Isaac Gym simulator to ensure the stability of all generated grasps under various gravity conditions.
paper_summary.summary: DexGraspNet is a large-scale dataset featuring 1.32 million dexterous grasps for 5355 diverse objects, generated for the ShadowHand using an improved simulation pipeline 50 times faster than prior methods. This dataset demonstrates superior diversity and stability compared to existing benchmarks, leading to consistent performance improvements for learning-based grasping algorithms.

title: DexGraspNet: A Large-Scale Robotic Dexterous Grasp Dataset for General Objects Based on Simulation

abstract: Task-oriented dexterous grasping holds broad application prospects in robotic manipulation and human-object interaction. However, most existing methods still struggle to generalize across diverse objects and task instructions, as they heavily rely on costly labeled data to ensure task-specific semantic alignment. In this study, we propose \textbf{ZeroDexGrasp}, a zero-shot task-oriented dexterous grasp synthesis framework integrating Multimodal Large Language Models with grasp refinement to generate human-like grasp poses that are well aligned with specific task objectives and object affordances. Specifically, ZeroDexGrasp employs prompt-based multi-stage semantic reasoning to infer initial grasp configurations and object contact information from task and object semantics, then exploits contact-guided grasp optimization to refine these poses for physical feasibility and task alignment. Experimental results demonstrate that ZeroDexGrasp enables high-quality zero-shot dexterous grasping on diverse unseen object categories and complex task requirements, advancing toward more generalizable and intelligent robotic grasping.

keyInsights: Grasp properties can be effectively decoupled: global hand positions and grasp types are primarily driven by high-level task semantics, while fine-grained finger configurations are influenced by local object geometry.

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can serve as powerful zero-shot reasoning engines, inferring abstract semantic components of a grasp by leveraging their broad understanding of commonsense knowledge and spatial relationships.

Combining MLLM-driven semantic inference with a subsequent geometry-guided physical optimization is crucial to bridge the gap between abstract task instructions and concrete, physically plausible dexterous hand poses.
originalProblem: Existing task-oriented dexterous grasping methods struggle with generalization to novel objects and tasks due to their heavy reliance on costly, manually labeled training data.
A significant gap persists between high-level natural language task instructions and the precise, low-level robotic actions required for high-degree-of-freedom dexterous hands.
Ensuring both semantic alignment with task instructions and physical feasibility for complex multi-finger grasps remains a major challenge in open-set scenarios.
results: ZeroDexGrasp achieved significantly reduced interpenetration (P.Vol. of 3.322 cm³ and P.Dep. of 0.358 cm) compared to the best baseline (P.Vol. 7.846 cm³, P.Dep. 1.437 cm), indicating higher physical plausibility.
Demonstrated superior semantic alignment, with a Part Accuracy of 90.075% and Semantic Contact Ratio of 83.831%, nearly doubling the performance of existing methods in zero-shot scenarios.

Successfully validated in real-world experiments using a Kinova Gen3 arm with Inspire FTP and RoboSense Papert dexterous hands, executing generated grasps on physical objects from single RGB-D inputs.
solution: The framework employs prompt-based multi-stage semantic reasoning using MLLMs to infer initial hand contact regions (part- and point-level), global hand position, grasp type, and rotation based on task instructions and object visuals.
It utilizes a geometry-guided verification step to filter physically implausible initial hand orientations before MLLM inference for rotation.

A contact-guided grasp refinement component optimizes the MLLM-inferred initial poses through an energy minimization process, ensuring physical feasibility, stability, minimal penetration, and high-fidelity semantic alignment.
paper_summary.summary: ZeroDexGrasp presents a framework for zero-shot task-oriented dexterous grasp synthesis, leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for multi-stage semantic reasoning combined with geometric optimization. This approach generates physically plausible and semantically aligned grasps for diverse, unseen objects and task instructions, outperforming baselines in accuracy and stability.

title: ZeroDexGrasp: Zero-Shot Task-Oriented Dexterous Grasp Synthesis with Prompt-Based Multi-Stage Semantic Reasoning

abstract: Learning dexterous bimanual manipulation policies critically depends on large-scale, high-quality demonstrations, yet current paradigms face inherent trade-offs: teleoperation provides physically grounded data but is prohibitively labor-intensive, while simulation-based synthesis scales efficiently but suffers from sim-to-real gaps. We present BiDemoSyn, a framework that synthesizes contact-rich, physically feasible bimanual demonstrations from a single real-world example. The key idea is to decompose tasks into invariant coordination blocks and variable, object-dependent adjustments, then adapt them through vision-guided alignment and lightweight trajectory optimization. This enables the generation of thousands of diverse and feasible demonstrations within several hour, without repeated teleoperation or reliance on imperfect simulation. Across six dual-arm tasks, we show that policies trained on BiDemoSyn data generalize robustly to novel object poses and shapes, significantly outperforming recent strong baselines. Beyond the one-shot setting, BiDemoSyn naturally extends to few-shot-based synthesis, improving object-level diversity and out-of-distribution generalization while maintaining strong data efficiency. Moreover, policies trained on BiDemoSyn data exhibit zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer to new robotic platforms, enabled by object-centric observations and a simplified 6-DoF end-effector action representation that decouples policies from embodiment-specific dynamics. By bridging the gap between efficiency and real-world fidelity, BiDemoSyn provides a scalable path toward practical imitation learning for complex bimanual manipulation without compromising physical grounding.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

title: One-Shot Real-World Demonstration Synthesis for Scalable Bimanual Manipulation

abstract:
Dexterous robotic hands enable versatile interactions due to the flexibility and adaptability of multi-fingered designs, allowing for a wide range of task-specific grasp configurations in diverse environments. However, to fully exploit the capabilities of dexterous hands, access to diverse and high-quality grasp data is essential -- whether for developing grasp prediction models from point clouds, training manipulation policies, or supporting high-level task planning with broader action options. Existing approaches for dataset generation typically rely on sampling-based algorithms or simplified force-closure analysis, which tend to converge to power grasps and often exhibit limited diversity. In this work, we propose a method to synthesize large-scale, diverse, and physically feasible grasps that extend beyond simple power grasps to include refined manipulations, such as pinches and tri-finger precision grasps. We introduce a rigorous, differentiable energy formulation of force closure, implicitly defined through a Quadratic Program (QP). Additionally, we present an adjusted optimization method (MALA*) that improves performance by dynamically rejecting gradient steps based on the distribution of energy values across all samples. We extensively evaluate our approach and demonstrate significant improvements in both grasp diversity and the stability of final grasp predictions. Finally, we provide a new, large-scale grasp dataset for 5,700 objects from DexGraspNet, comprising five different grippers and three distinct grasp types.
Dataset and Code:this https URL
title: GraspQP: Differentiable Optimization of Force Closure for Diverse and Robust Dexterous Grasping

abstract: Robotic grasping is a crucial task in industrial automation, where robots are increasingly expected to handle a wide range of objects. However, a significant challenge arises when robot grasping models trained on limited datasets encounter novel objects. In real-world environments such as warehouses or manufacturing plants, the diversity of objects can be vast, and grasping models need to generalize to this diversity. Training large, generalizable robot-grasping models requires geometrically diverse datasets. In this paper, we introduce GraspFactory, a dataset containing over 109 million 6-DoF grasps collectively for the Franka Panda (with 14,690 objects) and Robotiq 2F-85 grippers (with 33,710 objects). GraspFactory is designed for training data-intensive models, and we demonstrate the generalization capabilities of one such model trained on a subset of GraspFactory in both simulated and real-world settings. The dataset and tools are made available for download at this https URL.
title: GraspFactory: A Large Object-Centric Grasping Dataset

abstract: Humans naturally perform bimanual skills to handle large and heavy objects. To enhance robots' object manipulation capabilities, generating effective bimanual grasp poses is essential. Nevertheless, bimanual grasp synthesis for dexterous hand manipulators remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose the BimanGrasp algorithm for synthesizing bimanual grasps on 3D objects. The BimanGrasp algorithm generates grasp poses by optimizing an energy function that considers grasp stability and feasibility. Furthermore, the synthesized grasps are verified using the Isaac Gym physics simulation engine. These verified grasp poses form the BimanGrasp-Dataset, the first large-scale synthesized bimanual dexterous hand grasp pose dataset to our knowledge. The dataset comprises over 150k verified grasps on 900 objects, facilitating the synthesis of bimanual grasps through a data-driven approach. Last, we propose BimanGrasp-DDPM, a diffusion model trained on the BimanGrasp-Dataset. This model achieved a grasp synthesis success rate of 69.87\% and significant acceleration in computational speed compared to BimanGrasp algorithm.
title: Bimanual Grasp Synthesis for Dexterous Robot Hands

abstract: Robotic grasping from single-view observations remains a critical challenge in manipulation. Existing methods still struggle to generate stable and valid grasp poses when confronted with incomplete geometric information. To address these limitations, we propose SuperGrasp, a novel two-stage framework for single-view grasping with parallel-jaw grippers that decomposes the grasping process into initial grasp pose generation and subsequent grasp evaluation and refinement. In the first stage, we introduce a Similarity Matching Module that efficiently retrieves grasp candidates by matching the input single-view point cloud with a pre-computed primitive dataset based on superquadric coefficients. In the second stage, we propose E-RNet, an end-to-end network that expands the graspaware region and takes the initial grasp closure region as a local anchor region, enabling more accurate and reliable evaluation and refinement of grasp candidates. To enhance generalization, we construct a primitive dataset containing 1.5k primitives for similarity matching and collect a large-scale point cloud dataset with 100k stable grasp labels from 124 objects for network training. Extensive experiments in both simulation and realworld environments demonstrate that our method achieves stable grasping performance and strong generalization across varying scenes and novel objects.

keyInsights: Leveraging superquadric similarity matching to infer grasp points from known complete shapes significantly improves the quality and coverage of initial grasp candidates from incomplete point clouds.

Enlarging the grasp perception range and encoding a 'grasp anchor feature' from the original grasp closing region within E-RNet enhances the accuracy and robustness of grasp evaluation and feasibility prediction.

An explicit refinement branch that predicts the success likelihood of pre-defined pose adjustments (e.g., deepening, rotation) provides practical and effective pose correction, improving success rates in complex scenarios without exhaustive high-dimensional searching.
originalProblem: Generating stable and valid grasp poses from incomplete geometric information due to single-view observations in unstructured environments.
Existing superquadric-based methods struggle with shape and scale alignment from partial views, leading to unstable grasp candidates.

Data-driven two-stage methods produce unreliable grasp candidates from sparse, incomplete point clouds, impacting subsequent evaluation models, while end-to-end methods exhibit limited generalization to novel objects and require extensive training data.
results: Achieved higher Grasp Success Rates (GSR) and Task Success Rates (TSR) in simulation (e.g., 95.08% GSR in 10-object scenes) compared to PointNetGPD (78.76% GSR) and Contact-GraspNet (78.25% GSR).
Demonstrated strong generalization to unseen objects in simulation (89.03% GSR, 94.67% TSR) and novel objects in real-world environments (94.12% GSR, 96.00% TSR), surpassing baselines.
The total time cost for SuperGrasp was 0.789 seconds, faster than PointNetGPD (10.008 seconds), indicating an efficient framework given its performance gains.
solution: A two-stage framework: Superquadric Similarity Matching generates robust grasp candidates by matching single-view observations to a database of complete primitive shapes.
An E-RNet (Evaluation and Refinement Network) evaluates grasp feasibility and refines grasp poses, utilizing an enlarged perceptual context around the gripper's closing region.
A large-scale dataset of labeled grasp poses, including refined versions, was generated in a PyBullet simulation to train the E-RNet.
paper_summary.summary: SuperGrasp introduces a two-stage framework for stable single-view robotic grasping by employing superquadric similarity matching for robust grasp candidate generation and an end-to-end network for evaluating and refining grasp poses. The approach consistently outperformed baselines in simulation, achieving 95.08% Grasp Success Rate in 10-object scenes, and demonstrated strong generalization in real-world scenarios with 94.12% Grasp Success Rate for novel objects.

title: SuperGrasp: Single-View Object Grasping via Superquadric Similarity Matching, Evaluation, and Refinement

abstract: Dexterous grasping in cluttered environments presents substantial challenges due to the high degrees of freedom of dexterous hands, occlusion, and potential collisions arising from diverse object geometries and complex layouts. To address these challenges, we propose CADGrasp, a two-stage algorithm for general dexterous grasping using single-view point cloud inputs. In the first stage, we predict sparse IBS, a scene-decoupled, contact- and collision-aware representation, as the optimization target. Sparse IBS compactly encodes the geometric and contact relationships between the dexterous hand and the scene, enabling stable and collision-free dexterous grasp pose optimization. To enhance the prediction of this high-dimensional representation, we introduce an occupancy-diffusion model with voxel-level conditional guidance and force closure score filtering. In the second stage, we develop several energy functions and ranking strategies for optimization based on sparse IBS to generate high-quality dexterous grasp poses. Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world settings validate the effectiveness of our approach, demonstrating its capability to mitigate collisions while maintaining a high grasp success rate across diverse objects and complex scenes.

keyInsights: The sparse Interaction Bisector Surface (IBS) can serve as an effective scene-decoupled, embodiment-agnostic intermediate representation for dexterous grasping in cluttered environments with partial observations.

Explicitly modeling contact points (especially thumb vs. other fingers) and collision avoidance within the intermediate representation and optimization process significantly improves grasp stability and quality.

Combining a generative diffusion model for a high-dimensional intermediate representation with a gradient-based optimization for pose refinement offers a robust approach for tackling complex, high-DoF robotic tasks.
originalProblem: Performing stable and collision-free dexterous grasping in cluttered scenes with objects obscured by partial observations from a single camera view.
The high dimensionality of dexterous hand pose space and the sensitivity of physical constraints make direct end-to-end mapping from observations to grasp poses challenging.
Existing two-stage methods often rely on complete object geometries, limiting their applicability in cluttered, partially observed real-world environments.
results: Achieved a 93.8% grasp success rate in real-world cluttered scenes and 86.5% in dense simulated environments, outperforming prior methods.
Demonstrated zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, achieving a 77.6% success rate with an unseen Allegro hand using the same sparse IBS.
Improved computational efficiency, with an end-to-end inference time of 6.51 seconds, while providing superior grasp quality and diversity compared to baselines.
solution: A two-stage framework, CADGrasp, first predicts a sparse Interaction Bisector Surface (IBS) and then optimizes grasp poses.
The sparse IBS serves as a scene-decoupled, contact- and collision-aware intermediate representation, efficiently encoding geometric and contact relationships.

An occupancy-diffusion model generates sparse IBS candidates from single-view point clouds, followed by a gradient-based optimization guided by IBS constraints and tailored energy functions for stable, collision-free grasps.
paper_summary.summary: A two-stage framework, CADGrasp, enables general dexterous grasping in cluttered environments from single-view point clouds by leveraging a scene-decoupled, contact and collision-aware sparse Interaction Bisector Surface (IBS). The approach yielded an 86.5% grasp success rate in dense simulated scenes and 93.8% in real-world tests, also supporting zero-shot generalization to unseen robotic hands.

title: CADGrasp: Learning Contact and Collision Aware General Dexterous Grasping in Cluttered Scenes

abstract: Dexterous functional tool-use grasping is essential for effective robotic manipulation of tools. However, existing approaches face significant challenges in efficiently constructing large-scale datasets and ensuring generalizability to everyday object scales. These issues primarily arise from size mismatches between robotic and human hands, and the diversity in real-world object scales. To address these limitations, we propose the ScaleADFG framework, which consists of a fully automated dataset construction pipeline and a lightweight grasp generation network. Our dataset introduce an affordance-based algorithm to synthesize diverse tool-use grasp configurations without expert demonstrations, allowing flexible object-hand size ratios and enabling large robotic hands (compared to human hands) to grasp everyday objects effectively. Additionally, we leverage pre-trained models to generate extensive 3D assets and facilitate efficient retrieval of object affordances. Our dataset comprising five object categories, each containing over 1,000 unique shapes with 15 scale variations. After filtering, the dataset includes over 60,000 grasps for each 2 dexterous robotic hands. On top of this dataset, we train a lightweight, single-stage grasp generation network with a notably simple loss design, eliminating the need for post-refinement. This demonstrates the critical importance of large-scale datasets and multi-scale object variant for effective training. Extensive experiments in simulation and on real robot confirm that the ScaleADFG framework exhibits strong adaptability to objects of varying scales, enhancing functional grasp stability, diversity, and generalizability. Moreover, our network exhibits effective zero-shot transfer to real-world objects. Project page is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Utilizing pre-trained foundation models for 3D asset generation and affordance retrieval provides an effective and scalable solution for constructing large, diverse datasets for robotic manipulation.
Explicitly incorporating wide object scale variations into training data is crucial for developing grasp models that exhibit robust generalization to unseen object sizes in real-world scenarios.

An affordance-based grasp synthesis approach, combined with efficient optimization and a lightweight prediction network, enables strong zero-shot transfer from synthetic training data to real-world functional grasping tasks.
originalProblem: Existing methods for generating dexterous functional grasp datasets are labor-intensive, resulting in limited data volume and diversity, which hinders the training of robust deep learning models.

Robotic grasping solutions lack generalizability to the wide range of object scales encountered in real-world environments, often failing for objects outside a narrow size range due to data scarcity in scale variations.
results: The ScaleADFG dataset comprises over 60,000 functional grasps per hand across 5 object categories, each with 15 scale variations, demonstrating high average stability (91.60%) and functional ratio (81.20%) in human evaluations.
ScaleADFG-Net, trained on the synthetic dataset, achieved a 94.58% functional grasping success rate in comparative benchmarks and showed robust zero-shot transfer capabilities to real-world objects.

Real robot experiments with an Allegro Hand yielded an overall grasp success rate of 76.0% and a functional success rate of 58.7% across 15 novel objects, maintaining consistent performance across small, medium, and large object scales.
solution: An automated pipeline was developed to generate a large-scale, multi-hand, and scale-variant dataset by leveraging pre-trained foundation models for 3D asset creation and affordance retrieval.
An affordance-based optimization method, incorporating an axis-alignment initialization strategy, synthesizes functional grasps that adapt to various object scales for multiple dexterous hands.
ScaleADFG-Net, a lightweight Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE), was designed for efficient, generalizable, and scale-adaptive grasp prediction from the generated data.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from CASIA, CasiaHand Robotics, and SenseTime developed ScaleADFG, an affordance-based framework for dexterous functional grasping that includes a large-scale, multi-hand, scale-variant dataset. This approach automates data generation using foundation models and demonstrates robust zero-shot transfer to real-world objects, achieving a 76.0% grasp success rate on a real robot.

title: ScaleADFG: Affordance-based Dexterous Functional Grasping via Scalable Dataset

abstract: Cross-embodiment dexterous grasp synthesis refers to adaptively generating and optimizing grasps for various robotic hands with different morphologies. This capability is crucial for achieving versatile robotic manipulation in diverse environments and requires substantial amounts of reliable and diverse grasp data for effective model training and robust generalization. However, existing approaches either rely on physics-based optimization that lacks human-like kinematic understanding or require extensive manual data collection processes that are limited to anthropomorphic structures. In this paper, we propose CEDex, a novel cross-embodiment dexterous grasp synthesis method at scale that bridges human grasping kinematics and robot kinematics by aligning robot kinematic models with generated human-like contact representations. Given an object's point cloud and an arbitrary robotic hand model, CEDex first generates human-like contact representations using a Conditional Variational Auto-encoder pretrained on human contact data. It then performs kinematic human contact alignment through topological merging to consolidate multiple human hand parts into unified robot components, followed by a signed distance field-based grasp optimization with physics-aware constraints. Using CEDex, we construct the largest cross-embodiment grasp dataset to date, comprising 500K objects across four gripper types with 20M total grasps. Extensive experiments show that CEDex outperforms state-of-the-art approaches and our dataset benefits cross-embodiment grasp learning with high-quality diverse grasps.

keyInsights: Leveraging human grasping kinematics and adaptively merging them to robot morphologies enables the generation of grasps that are both physically stable and kinematically plausible across diverse robotic hands.

A hybrid approach, combining learned human-like contact representations with physics-aware optimization, is effective for synthesizing stable and diverse grasps at scale for various robot embodiments.
Large-scale cross-embodiment datasets that uniquely integrate human-like kinematics and physical awareness are crucial for advancing and evaluating universal robotic manipulation capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing dexterous grasping methods are often tailored to specific robotic hands, limiting their generalization across diverse robot morphologies.
Developing large, high-quality cross-embodiment grasp datasets that simultaneously incorporate human-like kinematic plausibility and physical stability remains a significant challenge.

Many physics-based optimization methods neglect human grasping kinematics, leading to stable but kinematically unnatural grasps, while human demonstration methods are labor-intensive and difficult to scale.
results: CEDex constructed the largest cross-embodiment grasp dataset to date, containing 20 million grasps for 500K objects across four diverse gripper types, which uniquely combines human-like kinematics and physical awareness.

The method achieved an average grasp success rate of 88.7% and a diversity of 0.512 rad. across three robotic hands, outperforming state-of-the-art cross-embodiment synthesis methods, with an average inference time of 0.12 seconds per grasp.

Training the learning-based DRO-Grasp network on the CEDex dataset improved its success rate by 3.5% (from 87.5% to 91.0%) and diversity by 2.2%, validating the dataset's high quality and effectiveness for advanced learning models.
solution: A two-stage approach combines a Conditional Variational Auto-encoder to generate object-centric human contact representations (contact and part maps).

Kinematic Human Contact Alignment adapts these human-like contacts to diverse robotic hand components using a predefined mapping, topological merging, and Signed Distance Field (SDF)-based optimization.

Physics-aware constraints, including Surface Pulling Force, External-Penetration Repulsion Force, and Self-Penetration Repulsion Force, are integrated during grasp optimization to ensure physical stability and avoid unrealistic configurations.
paper_summary.summary: The CEDex framework generates dexterous robotic grasps by integrating human-like contact representations with robot kinematics and physics-aware constraints. It has produced the largest cross-embodiment grasp dataset to date, featuring 20 million grasps for 500K objects across four diverse grippers, and consistently achieves superior grasp success rates and diversity compared to existing methods.

title: CEDex: Cross-Embodiment Dexterous Grasp Generation at Scale from Human-like Contact Representations

abstract: Bimanual grasping is essential for robots to handle large and complex objects. However, existing methods either focus solely on single-arm grasping or employ separate grasp generation and bimanual evaluation stages, leading to coordination problems including collision risks and unbalanced force distribution. To address these limitations, we propose BiGraspFormer, a unified end-to-end transformer framework that directly generates coordinated bimanual grasps from object point clouds. Our key idea is the Single-Guided Bimanual (SGB) strategy, which first generates diverse single grasp candidates using a transformer decoder, then leverages their learned features through specialized attention mechanisms to jointly predict bimanual poses and quality scores. This conditioning strategy reduces the complexity of the 12-DoF search space while ensuring coordinated bimanual manipulation. Comprehensive simulation experiments and real-world validation demonstrate that BiGraspFormer consistently outperforms existing methods while maintaining efficient inference speed (<0.05s), confirming the effectiveness of our framework. Code and supplementary materials are available at this https URL

organization_info.name: Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology

title: BiGraspFormer: End-to-End Bimanual Grasp Transformer

abstract:
Dexterous grasping is fundamental to robotics, yet data-driven grasp prediction heavily relies on large, diverse datasets that are costly to generate and typically limited to a narrow set of gripper morphologies. Analytical grasp synthesis can be used to scale data collection, but necessary simplifying assumptions often yield physically infeasible grasps that need to be filtered in high-fidelity simulators, significantly reducing the total number of grasps and their diversity.
We propose a scalable generate-and-refine pipeline for synthesizing large-scale, diverse, and physically feasible grasps. Instead of using high-fidelity simulators solely for verification and filtering, we leverage them as an optimization stage that continuously improves grasp quality without discarding precomputed candidates. More specifically, we initialize an evolutionary search with a seed set of analytically generated, potentially suboptimal grasps. We then refine these proposals directly in a high-fidelity simulator (Isaac Sim) using an asynchronous, gradient-free evolutionary algorithm, improving stability while maintaining diversity. In addition, this refinement stage can be guided toward human preferences and/or domain-specific quality metrics without requiring a differentiable objective. We further distill the refined grasp distribution into a diffusion model for robust real-world deployment, and highlight the role of diversity for both effective training and during deployment. Experiments on a newly introduced Handles dataset and a DexGraspNet subset demonstrate that our approach achieves over 120 distinct stable grasps per object (a 1.7-6x improvement over unrefined analytical methods) while outperforming diffusion-based alternatives by 46-60\% in unique grasp coverage.

keyInsights: High-fidelity physics simulators can function as active, continuous optimization stages for grasp synthesis, fundamentally shifting their role from mere verification tools.

Gradient-free evolutionary algorithms offer an effective method for optimizing complex and non-differentiable, high-dimensional spaces, such as full dexterous grasp configurations in physics simulations.

Integrating mechanisms like density-aware selection and archive-based insertion is critical for preserving and enhancing grasp diversity during optimization, preventing convergence to a limited set of solutions.
originalProblem: Difficulty in synthesizing physically feasible, robust, and diverse dexterous grasps for multi-fingered hands due to limitations of analytical and data-driven methods.
Inefficiency of current "generate-then-filter" pipelines, where high-fidelity simulators are used only for verification, discarding most candidates without providing optimization feedback.
Challenges in direct optimization of full dexterous grasp configurations within non-differentiable and computationally expensive high-fidelity physics simulators.
results: Achieved a 1.7-6x improvement in distinct stable grasps (DSG@) over unrefined analytical methods, consistently generating over 120 unique, stable grasps per object.
Demonstrated effective preservation and enhancement of grasp diversity, increasing entropy across grasp configuration spaces while improving stability.
Validated real-world transferability through robust grasping on a physical Franka Panda arm with an XHand, and successfully steered grasp synthesis towards human-preferred configurations.
solution: A scalable "generate-and-refine" pipeline using a gradient-free, asynchronous evolutionary algorithm to optimize dexterous grasps directly within high-fidelity physics simulators like Isaac Sim.
Integration of archive-based insertion and density-aware selection to actively preserve and enhance grasp diversity throughout the evolutionary refinement process.
Employment of a flexible fitness function capable of incorporating non-differentiable elements, such as human preferences or task-specific quality metrics, to guide grasp synthesis.
paper_summary.summary: ETH Zürich researchers developed DexEvolve, an evolutionary optimization framework that leverages high-fidelity physics simulators as an active refinement stage to synthesize robust and diverse dexterous grasps. This approach achieved a 1.7-6x improvement in distinct stable grasps over unrefined analytical methods and demonstrated successful real-world deployment and human-preference alignment.

title: DexEvolve: Evolutionary Optimization for Robust and Diverse Dexterous Grasp Synthesis

abstract:
Multi-hand semantic grasp generation aims to generate feasible and
semantically appropriate grasp poses for different robotic hands based on
natural language instructions. Although the task is highly valuable, due to the
lack of multihand grasp datasets with fine-grained contact description between
robotic hands and objects, it is still a long-standing difficult task. In this
paper, we present Multi-GraspSet, the first large-scale multi-hand grasp
dataset with automatically contact annotations. Based on Multi-GraspSet, we
propose Multi-GraspLLM, a unified language-guided grasp generation framework,
which leverages large language models (LLM) to handle variable-length
sequences, generating grasp poses for diverse robotic hands in a single unified
architecture. Multi-GraspLLM first aligns the encoded point cloud features and
text features into a unified semantic space. It then generates grasp bin tokens
that are subsequently converted into grasp pose for each robotic hand via
hand-aware linear mapping. The experimental results demonstrate that our
approach significantly outperforms existing methods in both real-world
experiments and simulator. More information can be found on our project page
this https URL
title: Multi-GraspLLM: A Multimodal LLM for Multi-Hand Semantic Guided Grasp  Generation

abstract:
In this work, we tackle the problem of learning universal robotic dexterous
grasping from a point cloud observation under a table-top setting. The goal is
to grasp and lift up objects in high-quality and diverse ways and generalize
across hundreds of categories and even the unseen. Inspired by successful
pipelines used in parallel gripper grasping, we split the task into two stages:
1) grasp proposal (pose) generation and 2) goal-conditioned grasp execution.
For the first stage, we propose a novel probabilistic model of grasp pose
conditioned on the point cloud observation that factorizes rotation from
translation and articulation. Trained on our synthesized large-scale dexterous
grasp dataset, this model enables us to sample diverse and high-quality
dexterous grasp poses for the object point cloud.For the second stage, we
propose to replace the motion planning used in parallel gripper grasping with a
goal-conditioned grasp policy, due to the complexity involved in dexterous
grasping execution. Note that it is very challenging to learn this highly
generalizable grasp policy that only takes realistic inputs without oracle
states. We thus propose several important innovations, including state
canonicalization, object curriculum, and teacher-student distillation.
Integrating the two stages, our final pipeline becomes the first to achieve
universal generalization for dexterous grasping, demonstrating an average
success rate of more than 60\% on thousands of object instances, which
significantly outperforms all baselines, meanwhile showing only a minimal
generalization gap.

keyInsights: Decoupling the complex distribution of dexterous grasp poses into a probabilistic model for hand root rotation and another for translation and articulation improves grasp quality and diversity.
A teacher-student learning framework, enhanced by state canonicalization and object curriculum learning, allows for a robust, generalizable vision-based execution policy.
Test-time optimization of generated grasp poses using a contact prediction network is crucial for achieving high physical plausibility and reducing penetration.
originalProblem: Robotic parallel grippers offer high success rates but lack the dexterity for intricate object manipulation.
Dexterous hands, while versatile, present high-dimensional control challenges for generating valid grasp poses and executing trajectories.
Existing dexterous grasping methods often require full object state information or fail to generalize universally to unseen objects from realistic visual inputs.
results: Synthesized the largest dexterous grasp dataset to date, containing 1.12 million grasps across 5,519 object instances.
The grasp proposal stage achieved the highest quality (Q1 0.0322 on unseen objects) and diversity, outperforming baselines.
The vision-based execution policy achieved a 63% success rate on unseen object categories, demonstrating robust generalization.
Showed a proof-of-concept for language-guided grasp selection with approximately 90% accuracy.
solution: A two-stage pipeline: dexterous grasp proposal generation followed by goal-conditioned grasp execution.
Grasp proposals are generated using a probabilistic model (GraspIPDF and GraspGlow) that factorizes rotation from translation and articulation, refined by ContactNet.
A vision-based execution policy is learned via a teacher-student framework, employing state canonicalization, object curriculum learning, and joint learning with object classification.
paper_summary.summary: UniDexGrasp introduces a system for universal robotic dexterous grasping from point cloud observations, generating diverse and high-quality grasps for thousands of seen and unseen objects. It achieves a 63% success rate on unseen object categories with its vision-based execution policy and provides the largest-scale dexterous grasp dataset with 1.12 million valid grasps.

title:
UniDexGrasp: Universal Robotic Dexterous Grasping via Learning Diverse
  Proposal Generation and Goal-Conditioned Policy

abstract:
Generating dexterous grasping has been a long-standing and challenging
robotic task. Despite recent progress, existing methods primarily suffer from
two issues. First, most prior arts focus on a specific type of robot hand,
lacking the generalizable capability of handling unseen ones. Second, prior
arts oftentimes fail to rapidly generate diverse grasps with a high success
rate. To jointly tackle these challenges with a unified solution, we propose
GenDexGrasp, a novel hand-agnostic grasping algorithm for generalizable
grasping. GenDexGrasp is trained on our proposed large-scale multi-hand
grasping dataset MultiDex synthesized with force closure optimization. By
leveraging the contact map as a hand-agnostic intermediate representation,
GenDexGrasp efficiently generates diverse and plausible grasping poses with a
high success rate and can transfer among diverse multi-fingered robotic hands.
Compared with previous methods, GenDexGrasp achieves a three-way trade-off
among success rate, inference speed, and diversity. Code is available at
this https URL

organization_info.name: Beijing Institute of General Artificial Intelligence (BIGAI)

keyInsights: The object-centric contact map serves as an effective hand-agnostic intermediate representation for generalizable dexterous grasping, decoupling hand design from grasp generation.

The proposed aligned distance significantly improves the accuracy of contact map synthesis, leading to more physically plausible and higher-success-rate grasps compared to traditional Euclidean distance.
It is possible to achieve a competitive trade-off between grasp success rate, diversity, and inference speed while maintaining strong generalizability to unseen robotic hands.
originalProblem: Existing dexterous grasping methods are often hand-specific, requiring retraining for each new robot hand and lacking generalizability.
Hand-agnostic grasping approaches are typically very slow, taking minutes to generate a single grasp, making them impractical for real-time use.
Many current methods produce limited diversity in grasping poses, hindering a robot's adaptability to varied task requirements and environments.
results: GenDexGrasp achieves a high grasp success rate of 77.19% on the ShadowHand test split, comparable to state-of-the-art hand-aware and slow hand-agnostic methods.

The method generates diverse grasps with a score of 0.207 rad, outperforming deterministic hand-aware approaches, while maintaining an inference speed of 16.415 seconds, significantly faster than other generalizable methods.

Ablation studies confirm the critical role of the aligned distance, improving success rates (e.g., from 58.91% to 77.19% for ShadowHand), and demonstrate robust generalization to unseen hand structures with comparable performance to in-domain training.
solution: A hand-agnostic contact map is used as an intermediate representation, encoding desired contact points on an object's surface independent of specific hand kinematics.
A novel "aligned distance" metric is introduced to accurately synthesize contact maps, especially for thin-shell objects, by incorporating object surface normals to reduce ambiguity.

The approach uses a two-stage pipeline: a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) trained on the large-scale MultiDex dataset generates contact maps, followed by a differentiable optimization to conform an arbitrary hand to the map.
paper_summary.summary: GenDexGrasp, developed by researchers from BIGAI, Tsinghua University, and Peking University, introduces a pipeline for generating versatile and generalizable dexterous grasps for arbitrary robot hands. The method balances a 77.19% success rate, 0.207 rad grasp diversity, and 16.415 seconds inference speed, demonstrating strong generalization to unseen hand kinematics without retraining.

title: GenDexGrasp: Generalizable Dexterous Grasping

abstract: Efficiently generating grasp poses tailored to specific regions of an object is vital for various robotic manipulation tasks, especially in a dual-arm setup. This scenario presents a significant challenge due to the complex geometries involved, requiring a deep understanding of the local geometry to generate grasps efficiently on the specified constrained regions. Existing methods only explore settings involving table-top/small objects and require augmented datasets to train, limiting their performance on complex objects. We propose CGDF: Constrained Grasp Diffusion Fields, a diffusion-based grasp generative model that generalizes to objects with arbitrary geometries, as well as generates dense grasps on the target regions. CGDF uses a part-guided diffusion approach that enables it to get high sample efficiency in constrained grasping without explicitly training on massive constraint-augmented datasets. We provide qualitative and quantitative comparisons using analytical metrics and in simulation, in both unconstrained and constrained settings to show that our method can generalize to generate stable grasps on complex objects, especially useful for dual-arm manipulation settings, while existing methods struggle to do so.
title: Constrained 6-DoF Grasp Generation on Complex Shapes for Improved Dual-Arm Manipulation

abstract: Bimanual dexterous manipulation is a critical yet underexplored area in robotics. Its high-dimensional action space and inherent task complexity present significant challenges for policy learning, and the limited task diversity in existing benchmarks hinders general-purpose skill development. Existing approaches largely depend on reinforcement learning, often constrained by intricately designed reward functions tailored to a narrow set of tasks. In this work, we present a novel approach for efficiently learning diverse bimanual dexterous skills from abundant human demonstrations. Specifically, we introduce BiDexHD, a framework that unifies task construction from existing bimanual datasets and employs teacher-student policy learning to address all tasks. The teacher learns state-based policies using a general two-stage reward function across tasks with shared behaviors, while the student distills the learned multi-task policies into a vision-based policy. With BiDexHD, scalable learning of numerous bimanual dexterous skills from auto-constructed tasks becomes feasible, offering promising advances toward universal bimanual dexterous manipulation. Our empirical evaluation on the TACO dataset, spanning 141 tasks across six categories, demonstrates a task fulfillment rate of 74.59% on trained tasks and 51.07% on unseen tasks, showcasing the effectiveness and competitive zero-shot generalization capabilities of BiDexHD. For videos and more information, visit our project page this https URL.

keyInsights: Independent learning for each hand (IPPO) effectively acquires robust bimanual skills and adapts better to diverse tasks compared to a centralized PPO approach.

An explicit two-stage reward function with an initial alignment phase is critical for successful bimanual manipulation, particularly when combined with functional grasping centers derived from human demonstrations.

Distilling state-based teacher policies into a single vision-based student policy, conditioned on visual features like point clouds, enables competitive zero-shot generalization to tasks involving unseen objects.
originalProblem: Developing robotic bimanual dexterous manipulation skills faces challenges due to the high-dimensional action space and complex coordination requirements.
Existing reinforcement learning approaches often rely on manually designed, task-specific reward functions, limiting scalability and generalization across diverse tasks.
Current benchmarks offer limited task diversity, and teleoperation methods require continuous human intervention, hindering autonomous application.
results: BiDexHD-IPPO teacher policies achieved 98.71% Stage 1 success and 78.18% Stage 2 tracking rate on trained tasks.

The vision-based student policy, BiDexHD-IPPO+DAgger, demonstrated 99.38% Stage 1 success and 74.59% Stage 2 tracking on trained tasks, and notably 94.79% Stage 1 success and 53.71% Stage 2 tracking on tasks with entirely new objects.
Ablation studies confirmed that the explicit alignment stage and functional grasping centers were essential for successful policy learning, showing substantial performance drops without them.
solution: BiDexHD is a three-phase framework: automated task construction from human demonstrations in simulation, state-based multi-task reinforcement learning (teacher policies) using IPPO, and vision-based policy distillation (student policy) with DAgger.
A unified two-stage reward function (simulation alignment and trajectory tracking) guides teacher policies, leveraging pre-computed functional grasping centers for robust initial interactions.
The vision-based student policy processes object point clouds and robot proprioception, enabling generalization by learning from expert teacher actions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University developed BiDexHD, a framework that constructs diverse bimanual dexterous manipulation tasks from human demonstrations and learns policies via a teacher-student reinforcement learning paradigm. The system demonstrated high success rates on trained tasks and competitive zero-shot generalization to tasks involving previously unseen objects, addressing the need for scalable bimanual skill acquisition.

title: Learning Diverse Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation Skills from Human Demonstrations

abstract: Dexterous grasping in multi-object scene constitutes a fundamental challenge in robotic manipulation. Current mainstream grasping datasets predominantly focus on single-object scenarios and predefined grasp configurations, often neglecting environmental interference and the modeling of dexterous pre-grasp gesture, thereby limiting their generalizability in real-world applications. To address this, we propose DGS-Net, an end-to-end grasp prediction network capable of learning dense grasp configurations from single-view point clouds in multi-object scene. Furthermore, we propose a two-stage grasp data generation strategy that progresses from dense single-object grasp synthesis to dense scene-level grasp generation. Our dataset comprises 307 objects, 240 multi-object scenes, and over 350k validated grasps. By explicitly modeling grasp offsets and pre-grasp configurations, the dataset provides more robust and accurate supervision for dexterous grasp learning. Experimental results show that DGS-Net achieves grasp success rates of 88.63\% in simulation and 78.98\% on a real robotic platform, while exhibiting lower penetration with a mean penetration depth of 0.375 mm and penetration volume of 559.45 mm^3, outperforming existing methods and demonstrating strong effectiveness and generalization capability. Our dataset is available at this https URL.
title: End-to-End Dexterous Grasp Learning from Single-View Point Clouds via a Multi-Object Scene Dataset

abstract: Dexterous grasp generation is a fundamental challenge in robotics, requiring both grasp stability and adaptability across diverse objects and tasks. Analytical methods ensure stable grasps but are inefficient and lack task adaptability, while generative approaches improve efficiency and task integration but generalize poorly to unseen objects and tasks due to data limitations. In this paper, we propose a transfer-based framework for dexterous grasp generation, leveraging a conditional diffusion model to transfer high-quality grasps from shape templates to novel objects within the same category. Specifically, we reformulate the grasp transfer problem as the generation of an object contact map, incorporating object shape similarity and task specifications into the diffusion process.
